channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: The meaning of ritual
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
The festival season is upon us and it’s hard to escape the hard sell from commercial establishments of all kinds – buy this, buy that, dhamaka offer this, special discount that. On the one hand, it’s touted to be a time when we reflect with gratitude for what we have, and take pleasure in our social bonds and cultural heritage. But on the other, it’s about giving in to our material desires, whether for simpler things like clothes and trinkets, or for the big budget items like jewellery and cars. Somewhere lost in all of this is the festival itself – what it represents, what it celebrates, and what it means.

But then, within the sanctuary of our homes and our families and communities, if we block out the aggressive marketing, there are ways in which we can rediscover that essence. We clean our homes, we light lamps, we share sweets with neighbours, and we spend time with family. The religious among us may perform traditional acts of worship or visit temples. Personally, I don’t generally set much store by ritual, and I recall as a child I almost always rebelled against doing something simply because “it has always been done” or because “it is good to do it”. The resistance came from not being given the reason why something had to be done, why it had become a practice, and why it was still relevant to my life. But as I’ve grown older, I have come to understand that some rituals do make sense, that they are not “empty” acts of habit but can provide a structure that helps contain other, necessary activities. So much of our everyday depends on habitual actions that can be dismissed as mere ritual. But such rituals can help us focus on the moment in a way that can be meaningful.

Take, for instance, morning assembly in school. We’re asked to stand in line, follow a pattern of behaviour, participate in some shared commitments – national anthem, school pledge, etc. Even if we disagree with the content of some of these “mantras”, their performance marks the beginning of the school day, a passage from home life to a period of learning. Ringing the bell is a ritual that marks the separation of one unit of learning from the next, giving students a signal that it’s time to shift gears.

Holiday rituals can sometimes become empty performances, but they also hold the possibility of recovering meaning. This may be why, even as I continue to question the relevance of some rituals, I am reluctant to give them up, sometimes, for purely aesthetic reasons. Who can, after all, deny the beauty of oil lamps lining a balcony ledge on Deepavali, the harmony of carols sung at Christmas, the aroma and flavour of sheer khorma at Eid, or the colourful kolam patterns on wet earth at Sankranti? These are rituals too, but in the doing of them we discover something, we feel something.
item.excerpt:encoded: The festival season is upon us and it’s hard to escape the hard sell from commercial establishments of all kinds – buy this, buy that, dhamaka offer this, special discount that.

item.title: The attention deficit conundrum
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Surabhi Agarwal

A teacher’s role has an evolving job description. Think of the teachers a decade ago and compare their approach with that of the current generation of educators. Even though their task remains the same, the methods, mannerisms, and attitude have evolved. Educators today need to learn, unlearn, apply, adapt, own mistakes, get stuck, and find ways to get the concepts across. With the diminishing attention spans of students, this role is now more challenging than it ever was.

Students have quick and easy access to short-form content like videos, reels, news, and social media. If they don’t find the need to engage with it, they’ll most likely scroll to the next one. They increasingly lack the patience to stay with a piece, dig deep, and ask questions. Unfortunately, the teaching-learning process has also fallen victim to the pandemic of shrinking attention span.

Teachers find it difficult to hold their students’ attention long enough for them to derive any learning from the lessons being taught. This article explores the research around classroom attention deficit and the ways of tackling it. It simplifies the jargon used in psychological research and condenses information into digestible chunks. It is broken into four sections for easy reading.

  • Spell it – What is attention deficit?
  • Diagnose it – What does an inattentive classroom look like?
  • Understand it – Why are students distracted in the class?
  • Hold it – What strategies can be used to keep the class focused?

Spell it

What is attention deficit?

It is not difficult for professionals to earmark distracted behaviour. Recall being a member of an audience for a meeting, session, or training where your attention drifted away. Wouldn’t the same hold true for a class full of learners? While it may be natural to occasionally zone out, a good facilitator will try to address the reasons behind it. But before we analyze the problem and look for solutions, we first need to define it.

Diagnose it

What does an inattentive classroom look like?

Teachers are the best judges of learning, because they are aware of the gap between what was planned and how it is playing out. However, there are a few symptoms that might point to the lack of their class’s attention. Note that such symptoms must be observed in more than one learner over a stretch of time. Also, they must be studied in the context of the grade and subject being analyzed. Here are key indicators that reflect an inattentive classroom.

  • Frequent disruptions: Students ask for frequent water or washroom breaks, talking amongst themselves, fidgeting, or intentionally disrupting peers.
  • Lack of eye contact: They avoid eye contact during direct instruction or a personal intervention.
  • Curriculum pace: Teachers frequently miss meeting the lesson’s objectives and spend significant time in managing spillovers.
  • Repeated errors in student work: Teachers don’t give adequate and meaningful feedback to the students for their work. In such a case, students make repetitive mistakes (spelling errors, conceptual errors).
  • Uneven class response: Few and a fixed set of students respond to the teacher, while the rest either avoid participation or give incorrect responses.
  • Passive learning: Students show reluctance and unwillingness to be a part of the learning process. They don’t ask questions or engage with peers.
  • Ineffective activities: They have trouble following instructions or in understanding how to sequentially complete a task.
  • Zoning out: Students are lost in their own thoughts or are gazing out.
  • Consistently low results: The class shows low productivity for projects and assignments, possibly due to lack of understanding/interest. Assignments are erroneous and below the expected quality.
  • Forgetfulness: Students are unable to answer simple questions about recently taught material.
  • Anxiety: Students are stressed to answer and nervous when asked to participate.
  • Absenteeism: Students regularly and consistently miss classes reflecting their disinterest in learning.

Understand it

Why are students distracted in class?

As an instructor, having an audience that is unwilling to learn and showing signs of distraction can get overwhelming and unmanageable. Understanding a few facts about attention span helps teachers in managing their own expectations.

Attention span is a stretch of sustained and uninterrupted focus on a given task. Psychologists suggest that it is considerably low in children as compared to adults. A 2-year-old has an attention span of 4-6 minutes. On an average, it gradually increases by 2 minutes every year. This list is compiled as per the restructured 5+3+3+4 framework described in NEP 2020.

Source: https://www.cnld.org/how-long-should-a-childs-attention-span-be/

So, children getting distracted in a long lesson might be entirely age-appropriate! Nevertheless, the task of keeping students focused belongs to the teacher.

While some distractions are manageable by the facilitator, there are many that are internal to students and may need school intervention. For example, noise, use of devices, and peer behaviour are manageable distractions and can be easily handled. Whereas, personal issues, emotional challenges, and health concerns (hunger, lack of sleep, exhaustion) are beyond the teacher’s control. These need to be addressed through wider school intervention or personal counselling.

Hold it

What strategies can be used to keep the class focused?

“Research shows that students cannot learn as well in classrooms that lack consistency, have too many potential distractions or do not offer ample opportunities to engage.”

A good lesson will aim to minimize these distractions and maximize active student engagement. Here, I elaborate a few research-driven teaching strategies that can be used to increase attention span. Based on the teaching goal, these strategies have been categorized into four groups – structural, content, pedagogical, and behavioural. They need to be contextualized based on the grade, subject, concept, or activity.

  1. Structural
  • Establish routines: Routines allow students to know what to expect in an ongoing lesson. This means having a consistent pattern of actions for each learning stage such as introduction, guided practice, independent practice, and assessment. This predictability helps them build deep focus when required.

Take the case of a teacher who

  • begins every lesson with a recap of the previous learning,
    • explains the end goal of the lesson,
    • uses timer/alarm that shows the beginning and end of an activity,
    • wraps up with an exit slip having a fixed pattern of questions.

Such a scenario allows students to structure the learning curve and focus on the defined learning time without being overwhelmed by the content. Before the lesson begins, they will know that the first and last few minutes are for reinforcement and the middle section is where they get introduced to new concepts and require undivided attention.

  • Apply rules consistently: Having a few clear, positively framed, and consistent rules will discourage behaviour that causes distraction. Take an example where the instructor is setting up for a group discussion.

“What are the three rules we follow during group discussions? Raise hands to speak, share crisp points, and no interruptions.”

Stating these three clear and easy-to-follow rules will curb potential chaos of a discussion-based activity.

To successfully carry out a rules-based classroom, it is important to apply them consistently to all students without exceptions. It helps them understand that

  • they are all being held to the same standard,
  • following the rules is essential for learning.

For higher grades,invite their opinions and involve them in making class-wide rules.

  • Eliminate distractions: Have a meaningful and defined classroom layout for each activity that
  • uses the space efficiently,
  • saves learning time that gets utilized in setting up,
  • reduces distractions caused by spaces that are too cluttered or too empty.

For example, use row-wise seating while using the blackboard/smartboard. Use a grouped layout for activities, discussions, and projects. As far as feasible, minimize disruptions from any external or internal school noise.

2. Content

Use meaningful Teaching Learning Materials (TLMs) – TLMs refer to the teaching aids being used in the lesson. These could include worksheets, assessments, handouts, visual aids, and digital content. One of the most common causes of loss of focus is that the TLMs are not meaningful, interesting, or relatable for students.

Read the following word problem from a grade 6 math worksheet:

A shopkeeper has 576 apples. He sells 238 apples. How many apples are left with him?

Now, let us rephrase this word problem to make it relatable and interesting for a learner in grade 6.

Vishal and Rohan were playing with building blocks. They had a set of 576 blocks of many shapes and sizes. Mom roughly distributed the blocks between them. Rohan counted 238 blocks with him.

a. Who had more blocks?

b. How many blocks did Vishal have?

c. Were the blocks distributed equally?

Note that the assessment objective has been kept intact (three-digit subtraction using a word problem). It may look the same, but from a student’s perspective, the rephrased question is relatable and immediately builds their interest in solving it accurately. It also gives them calibrated options with varying difficulty levels to express their understanding of the concept. Answering one sub-part builds confidence to solve the next one, thereby extending focus.

The same strategy can be applied while citing examples or demonstrating experiments. Key aspects of TLMs that help sustain focus are that they:

  • align with student interests,
  • have clear connection with lesson objectives and larger curricular goal,
  • avoid outdated examples and terminology,
  • provide choice for expressing content mastery,
  • use vocabulary at a difficulty level that students can grasp,
  • cite relatable examples, stories, experiments, and incidents.

Teachers must research student interest to create/curate content that is relevant, simple, and contextualized. Irrelevant and uninteresting TLMs may lead to frustration resulting in a lack of concentration. Dedicating time to adapt TLMs to student interest goes a long way in increasing their attention span.

3. Pedagogical

  • Plan with focus and flexibility – Attentive, pragmatic, and flexible lesson planning allows teachers to anticipate disruptions and maximize learning time. Planning must offer interactivity and engagement with the TLMs. Be cognizant of the rules and routines that need to be followed to conduct the lesson.
  • Include brain breaks: A typical lesson duration is 30-40 minutes, which is considerably longer than the attention span of students.
    • Chunk long tasks into small and manageable steps.
    • Include short breaks that allows the brain to refocus.
    • Incorporate movement and change of activities.
    • Use tried and tested attention grabbers to hold collective attention.
  • Create a mix of learning preferences: There are many accepted learning theories that are well-researched but often contradictory. Even if deciphered accurately, it is unlikely to customize content or pedagogy for each student’s learning style.

A more reasonable approach is to ensure a mix of learning styles while teaching. To engage effectively with content, include discussions, projects, worksheets, presentations, handouts, entry/exit slips, diagrams, role-playing, games, and music recordings. Use technology to enhance or extend learning.

4. Behavioural

  • Modulate body language: Teacher voice and tone must be calm, clear, audible, and respectful. They must strive to hold attention by maintaining eye contact while speaking. Use hand gestures and facial expressions to guide attention towards learning. A teacher’s enthusiasm reflects their own interest and passion for the subject and gets easily acquired by the learners.
  • Read the room: Sometimes, strong lesson plans fail and tested strategies don’t bring any results. This is because, in practice, teaching is a unique social interaction. It is difficult to put any theory into practice, unless the teacher is able to read the room and comprehend the collective mood of the class.

Many students face personal distress that makes it hard for them to stay focused during class. For example, hunger, sleep, physical, or emotional turmoil. Here, the teacher needs to take a multi-pronged approach and

  • address such issues through after-school interventions,
    • praise and encourage students to remain focused,
    • positively reinforce the rules and routines,
    • maintain calm in the face of sudden disruptions and uncertainty,
    • use language that is grade appropriate and positively framed.
  • Let small things be small: Sometimes, even the smallest action by one student can take an entire classroom off-track. Unless urgent, avoid using class learning time to tackle a small disturbance. Pin the corrective action and take it up after learning time is over. Provide them with options to deal with the loss of focus. For example, post-school counselling or extra time to the learner.

The elephant in the room

Lifestyle changes, short-form content, and a plethora of instantly available content have reduced the average attention span of both adults and children. All the theory and strategies in this article have assumed that the teacher is completely focused during the lesson. The one aspect of teaching that remains unaddressed is the lack of focus within teachers themselves.

This makes it all the more essential to assess lessons through observations and peer feedback. Educators must understand that collectively they are working with limited attention spans. It requires them to model the attentive behaviour that is expected of their students.

References

https://www.edresearch.edu.au/sites/default/files/2021-02/AERO-Tried-and-tested-guide-Focused-classrooms.pdf

https://www.researchgate.net/publication/326066230_Classroom_Strategies_for_Maintaining_Student_Focus

https://www.cnld.org/how-long-should-a-childs-attention-span-be/ (Center for Neuropsychology, Learning & Development)

Surabhi Agarwal is a former Teach for India fellow who works as an instructional designer and can be reached at surabhi.edtech@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Surabhi Agarwal
With countless distractions vying for children’s attention, teachers today face a tough challenge. Surabhi Agarwal shares some strategies to engage children’s dwindling attention.

item.title: Wild India safari
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mamata Pandya

National Parks are popular tourist attractions, so quite a few of us may have enjoyed the safaris that are organized to get a glimpse of some wildlife. Our visits are usually inspired by our desire and excitement to see the “stars” that these places are known for – a tiger, a lion, a rhino, or an elephant. However, these are not always ‘on display’, and in our disappointment at missing sighting them, we also miss out on the fascinating canvas of other flora and fauna that Nature has created and offers us in the same landscape – plants, birds, reptiles, and even insects, if only we care to observe and notice them.

These opportunities are available to us through our visits to Protected Areas that have been designated by the Government of India.

A Protected Area is a clearly defined geographic space, recognized, dedicated, and managed, through legal or other effective means to achieve long-term conservation of Nature with associated ecosystem services and cultural values.

India has several categories of Protected Areas (PAs)

National Park (NP): A tract of land declared the nation’s property by the central government with a view to preserve natural and cultural heritage, and where human pressures (hunting, fishing, extraction, residences, commercial activities) are not allowed.

Wildlife Sanctuary (WLS): An area designated usually by a state government, where it is illegal to interfere in any way with natural life. Hunting and fishing are prohibited; some activities are allowed with special permits.

Conservation Reserves: Areas which a state government may declare after consultations with relevant local communities. Areas adjacent to existing protected areas or those connecting one protected area to another would be prioritized.

Community Reserves: These are areas which the local community has volunteered to conserve on private or community land. These areas could be for the protection of fauna, flora, and traditional or cultural conservation values and practices.

As per the National Wildlife Information System (NWIS) on the Protected Areas of the country being developed by the National Wildlife Database Centre of Wildlife Institute of India (WII), India has a network of 1014 Protected Areas spread over approximately 5.32% of the country, which is about 1,75,169.42 km2 of its geographical area. This includes 106 National Parks, 573 Wildlife Sanctuaries, 115 Conservation Reserves and 220 Community Reserves.

In addition to these, India has a few more specially designated conservation areas including Tiger Reserves, Elephant Reserves, Ramsar Sites, Important Bird Areas, UNESCO Natural Heritage Sites, and Biosphere Reserves.

Let’s find out how much we know about these areas that help protect and conserve unique landscapes, flora, and fauna.

The next time you plan a holiday, find out which of these many Protected Areas are in your own state. Get started by taking this quiz.

Protected areas quiz

  1. India’s first national park was created in 1936. It was originally named Hailey National Park. By what name is this popular park now known?
  1. Ranthambore National Park
  2. Kanha National Park
  3. Jim Corbett National Park

2. The one-horned rhino is the star attraction in this park.

  1. Kaziranga National Park
  2. Namdapha National Park
  3. Manas National Park

3. The first marine national park in India was set up in 1982. Where is it located?

  1. Gulf of Kutch
  2. Gulf of Mannar
  3. Andaman and Nicobar Islands

4. The largest national park in India is

  1. Kanha National Park
  2. Corbett National Park
  3. Hemis National Park

5. Which animal will you be able to see in the Rann of Kutch Wildlife Sanctuary?

  1. Wild Ass
  2. Great Indian Bustard
  3. Spiny-tailed lizard

6. The Keibul Lamjao National park in Manipur is unique. Why?

  1. It is the only floating national park in the world.
  2. It is the home of the endangered brow-antlered deer or sangai
  3. It is located on the Loktak Lake which has a unique ecosystem called Phumdi (a Manipuri word meaning floating mats of soil and vegetation).

7. The Gahirmatha Turtle Sanctuary in Odisha is in the news when:

  1. Millions of Olive Ridley turtles come ashore to lay their eggs
  2. There are cyclones
  3. There is encroachment of the beach

8. Desert National Park near Jaisalmer in Rajasthan protects this endangered species

  1. Spiny-tailed lizard
  2. Great Indian Bustard
  3. Chinkara

9. Bhitarkanika is famous for its mangroves, estuarine ecosystem, and as home to the largest congregation of salt-water crocodiles in India. In which state is it located?

  1. Andhra Pradesh
  2. Odisha
  3. West Bengal

10. What do these have in common: The Valley of Flowers National Park, Nanda Devi National Park, Manas National Park

  1. They are all designated as UNESCO World Heritage Sites
  2. They are known for their flora
  3. They are located at high altitudes.

11. Keoladeo National Park is recognized as one of the world's most important bird breeding and feeding grounds. In which state is it located?

  1. Madhya Pradesh
  2. Haryana
  3. Rajasthan

12. South Button National Park in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands is the smallest National Park in India. What is its area?

  1. 100 sq km
  2. 50 sq km
  3. 5 sq km

13. Khangchendzonga National Park is home to nearly half of India's bird diversity, wild trees, orchids and rhododendrons, and one third of the country's flowering plants, and many species of endemic fauna. The area with a unique diversity of landscapes has deep cultural and spiritual significance for the local people. In which state is it located?

  1. Sikkim
  2. Arunachal Pradesh
  3. Mizoram

14. Guindy National park is one of the few national parks located within a city. In which city is it located?

  1. Bengaluru
  2. Chennai
  3. Hyderabad

15. The Great Himalayan National Park is spread across four valleys; the ecozones have large variations in altitude and geography ranging from the sub-tropical to the alpine, each offering a huge range of plant and animal species. Few ecological sanctuaries of similar size encompass such diversity. In which state is this located?

  1. Himachal Pradesh
  2. Kashmir
  3. Uttarakhand

Mamata Pandya worked at the Centre for Environment Education at Ahmedabad for over three decades, where she was engaged in developing creative teaching-learning resources for educators and children. She is now an independent consultant, editor, writer, translator, storyteller, and blogger. She can be reached at mamata.pandya@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mamata Pandya
From floating national parks in Manipur to desert sanctuaries in Rajasthan, Mamata Pandya presents a closer look at the Protected Areas and some unique parks and reserves in our country.

item.title: Curating culturally sensitive classrooms
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aruna Sankaranarayanan

As I transitioned from kindergarten to first grade, I remember being very excited by my Hindi textbook at the end of the summer vacation as my mother covered each book with brown paper. Though I could not decipher a single letter, I was enthralled by the colourful pages with letters and pictures. But my enthusiasm was short-lived as the first class was a dampener. The teacher, who spoke only in Hindi, was warm and receptive to children who answered her in Hindi. However, for students like me, who did not know a word of Hindi, the experience was rather daunting. Neither did I understand what the teacher was saying, nor did I dare to open my mouth. The teacher just ignored the silent ones and continued to engage with the Hindi-speaking children. She did not in any way reassure us that it was okay if some of us did not understand or speak the language.

The year carried on, with children who spoke Hindi at home being favoured by the teacher. For children like me, a fear of the language was instilled early on. Though the teacher was probably aware that the class consisted of children who spoke various Indian languages at home, she did not convey any sensitivity about these linguistic differences. As a scared six-year-old, I felt that I wasn’t good at Hindi. Unfortunately, that feeling continued to dog me through my school years, turning into a self-fulfilling prophecy.

Listen for diverse voices

In a country like ours, that houses multiple diversities, teachers need to understand the unique cultural contexts and backgrounds of each child. Language, caste, class, religion, gender and (dis)ability, intersect in myriad ways, crafting complex and nuanced identities. Children are more likely to learn and thrive only when they feel heard and understood. And, a skilled teacher is one who listens and responds to silences and lets quiet students know that they too are heard.

In Teaching to Transgress, bell hooks emphasizes that education that enhances freedom is vastly different from education that “strives to reinforce domination.” Since teachers automatically exert power over students, it is important that they connect with their pupils. According to hooks, teachers need to exhibit ‘interest’ in students, listen to their ‘voices’ and “value everyone’s presence.” In my first-grade Hindi class, all three elements were missing. The teacher did not evince interest in children who did not speak in class and our voices were not heard. Further, the non-Hindi speaking children were rendered invisible.

Address curricular biases

Further, hooks also writes that we need to look out for “biases in curricula” that reinforce systemic hierarchies. In a telephonic interview with Sriranjani, Coordinator, Kaigal Education & Environment Program, she echoes hooks’ sentiments that children need to feel welcome and valued. True inclusion cannot take place, avers Sriranjani, unless we “reimagine curricula” to reflect their lived experiences. The tribal children, whom she works with in rural Andhra Pradesh, have a deep ecological sensitivity. Subjects like maths and science can be more meaningful if we connect concepts to their “experiential knowledge”. We also have to contextualize problems based on their experiences. Rather than give them word problems centered around balls and balloons, we may contextualize the problems by using sticks and stones, thereby affirming what they know. Likewise, the natural surroundings may be harnessed to impart second languages that seem foreign and de-contextualized to them.

Further, Sriranjani avers that we need to eschew a top-down approach of curricular planning that does not provide room for locally relevant contextual modifications. Ideally, given broad guidelines, teachers should have the leeway to make changes to a curriculum, factoring in the local milieu and the unique cultural needs of the children in an area.

Just because a child does not speak a particular language or does not have the same experiences as those who are considered ‘mainstream,’ it does not mean that the child is deficient or is in need of fixing. Often, and sometimes even unknowingly, teachers may invalidate children’s lived realities. Education, for these children, then becomes something to be endured rather than cherished.

Every child is worthy

As hooks says, when education is approached as a “practice of freedom,” every student in the classroom feels that they are capable of learning. And for a teacher to practice an “engaged pedagogy,” the needs and wellbeing of the teaching faculty also have to be taken into account.

Further, cultural sensitivity also goes hand-in-hand with cultural humility, which Daryl Van Tongeren defines as “an openness to other people and their unique cultural perspectives.” In his book, Humble, Van Tongeren says that our ways of viewing and being in the world are not “inherently superior” and we need to exhibit curiosity towards other worldviews and life choices. Culturally humble people realize that each person is shaped by a complex interplay of umpteen forces and that every person’s story is unique. Further, they believe that diversity, in every so many guises, enhances and enriches the social fabric.

Cultural sensitivity is an ongoing process as we need to be aware and responsive to the changing dynamics of our social milieu. Van Torgeren also asks us to be especially attuned to the needs and voices of groups that have been “historically marginalized and oppressed.” Recognizing systemic inequalities and wanting to create a more equal and just world also encompasses cultural sensitivity.

At its core, cultural humility is about recognizing that every person or group, however different they may be, is inherently valuable. A truly inclusive classroom champions diversity in every so many ways, be it in terms of being, doing, or thinking, while making every child feel worthy.

References

  • hooks, b. (2020) Teaching to transgress: Education as the practice of freedom, New York: Routledge
  • Van Torgeren, D. (2022) Humble. London: Welbeck Balance

Aruna Sankaranarayanan is the author of Zero Limits and the co-author of Bee-Witched along with Brinda S. Narayan. She blogs at www.arunasankaranarayanan.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aruna Sankaranarayanan
Cultural sensitivity in education goes beyond celebrating diversity. Aruna Sankaranarayanan writes that it requires teachers to confront their own biases and recognize every child’s uniqueness.

item.title: Stop, look, and wonder: building a context-based learning curriculum
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ankita Rajasekharan and Shikha Nain

In a world that is rapidly changing, the need to reconnect with Nature has never been more urgent. Most children are natural explorers – they pause to watch an ant carry food, listen to the rustling leaves, and notice tiny details that adults often overlook. ‘Stop, Look and Wonder’, a contextual curriculum created by Earth Focus Foundation, is an invitation to nurture this curiosity – to encourage educators and children to slow down, observe their surroundings, and ask questions about the world they live in.

Rooted in the rich ecological and cultural landscape of the Kanha region, this curriculum is an effort to blend traditional wisdom with modern pedagogy, ensuring that learning is both contextual and experiential. This approach fosters a sense of wonder and discovery, helping children build a deeper, more personal connection with Nature. The essence of this curriculum lies in its ability to transform passive observation into active engagement. However, awareness alone is not enough. The goal is to move beyond understanding, from awareness to action – where children not only learn about the biodiversity surrounding them, but also participate in conservation efforts, becoming stewards of their environment.

Education is most impactful when it is relevant to a child’s lived experience. For generations, indigenous communities such as the Gond and Baiga, have lived in harmony with Nature. Their knowledge of the land, flora, and fauna has helped them foster a deep connection with the forest. However, in recent years, rapid urbanization, relocation, and the lack of contextual learning in schools have created a growing disconnection. Children who once grew up listening to the stories of the Mahua tree, the songs of birds, and the rhythm of the seasons now find themselves detached from their natural heritage. Our curriculum, Stop, Look, and Wonder, seeks to rebuild this connection by integrating local knowledge into structured learning environments.

For communities living close to forests, Nature has always been a part of daily life, but modern influences are changing this relationship. Many children grow up without truly seeing the forests, streams, and creatures around them. Schools often don’t have the right resources to help children explore the natural world in a way that feels real and personal. This curriculum offers a way to bridge that gap – helping children observe, engage, and feel a sense of belonging in the environment they grow up in. For the children of Kanha, Nature is not unfamiliar. They know their birds, trees, and the rhythms of the forest. However, there has been a growing disconnection – one that has distanced them from the respect and love for the land that their tribal communities hold. The forced relocation of many families and the absence of contextual learning in schools have further contributed to this disconnect. The forest, once a source of identity and sustenance, is now often viewed as something separate from daily life.

Conservation and ecological awareness cannot exist in isolation from culture and lived experiences. This curriculum is not just a biodiversity or science textbook, it is a bridge between what children see around them and the larger cultural and environmental systems they are part of. For example, rather than simply reading about different soil types, students engage in sensory activities – touching, observing, and even painting with soil. Instead of learning about biodiversity from the pages of a book, they explore forests, map their habitat, and record species. This place-based approach ensures that learning is not abstract but rather an extension of what children already know and experience.

The curriculum takes the form of an educator's handbook in both Hindi and English in its final version. It is designed as a flexible companion for educators, helping them spark curiosity, encourage observation, and nurture critical thinking in children. Whether used by teachers, community educators, or facilitators, this resource finds its place in classrooms, nature clubs, and community programs alike. Through hands-on activities, storytelling, and field explorations, it offers a learning experience that is both structured and adaptable – allowing educators to shape lessons based on their surroundings and the needs of their students.

The journey of the curriculum

What started as being called the ‘biodiversity curriculum’, came to be ‘a curriculum for context-based learning’. And in this journey from one name to another, much was discovered and uncovered about what we wanted to address in the curriculum we were building, where we saw the gaps in where children lived, what they were surrounded by and what they learnt about, the connections we wanted to rekindle, areas of observation, attention, and wonder that needed to be encouraged.

In a meeting, one of the education coordinators at Earth Focus Foundation dropped a question into the discussion, “How do we get children to care about the forests we are surrounded by, the forests that we used to live within?” This is where we began. It wasn’t that children did not know their forests or what species moved in them. It wasn’t that children needed to be introduced to their own forests. They were, after all, children of forest-dwelling communities, the adivasi communities that have thrived and lived in deep communion with these very forests. Somewhere along the way as they came to live more in the fringes of the forest rather than the heart of it, this connection and reverence for the forests has had to battle the attractions and conveniences of a consumerist and capitalist world.

We then imagined our curriculum to be an invitation to pause and look around. In stopping, we notice things that are otherwise a blur. In paying attention to what is around, we begin to hold questions about what we notice. In wondering about what we are surrounded by, we begin to care for it. Alongside the team of educators, we listed out the different areas of our everyday living that we wanted to pause and relook, in the context of the forest ecosystem. The themes that surfaced included habitats of different species, feeding habits, and general behaviour, folklore and myths surrounding some species, fear of things in forests, knowing the names of small and big creatures, trees and flowers, sounds of the forest, interconnection among different species and ecosystems. Once we landed on our curiosities, we stepped out into the community to learn what they already knew of these themes. We came back with several stories, insights into ways of living and some more questions that we hadn’t begun asking yet!

We quickly realized that one curriculum wouldn’t do justice to all that the forest had to offer in terms of exploration, study, and wonderment. We settled into the idea of a multi-volume curriculum. We picked six themes to start with, which would be the first volume of our curriculum, ‘Stop, Look and Wonder’. Then came the work of studying and playing with these themes to create activities that’d nurture and enhance children’s inherent sense of curiosity. We created a ten-point checklist to ensure that the activities and lessons we designed kept the child at the centre of the experience. Some of these pointers included – does the activity have a sensorial element, are there open-ended, thinking questions in the discussion prompts, etc. Each activity was reviewed against this checklist first, then tried out within the team of educators, followed by a trial in the classroom, and finally having gone through the necessary critique and edits, it moved into the stage of illustration and design. It was very important that the visual aspect of the curriculum be reflective of the environmental context and the community that inspired and informed the content. For too long, textbooks and curriculums have been generic or representative of only a select context. We wanted the educators and children to be able to see themselves, their people, and their forest looking back at them in the book and worksheets.

The journey ahead

Our first volume of the educator’s handbook is already out in the field, and being used in the classrooms. In the first phase, we have the educators and shikhsha preraks (community educators) from within the organization executing the curriculum in government schools around the Kanha Tiger Reserve. We work with children prior to their formal schooling hours, between 8-10 a.m. It is completely voluntary, and we have 2800+ children from across 36 villages that have been participating in the program, Anandghar, receiving support and experiences in the areas of literacy, numeracy, and now biodiversity and context-based learning. While we have been receiving encouraging responses from the children of the Kanha forests, we are now keen on having children from other forest-communities experience and engage with the curriculum too. The home-base of the curriculum has been Kanha, the activities however, are easily replicable to other forest communities with a few adaptations to their unique contexts.

With the continued support of our partners, we have already begun working on the second volume of the context-based curriculum. We are looking to dive deeper this time into the intersection of the cultural and natural world of this landscape. We have themes including but not limited to myths and folklore, agricultural practices and biodiversity, the world of critters, and more! We’d love the company of more educators and community members along this journey. Our guiding principle remains to stop, look, and wonder, and in doing so we shall have entire worlds open up for us to reflect upon and come into our own in our humble place in the natural world.

A digital copy of the curriculum may be accessed at:  https://www.earthfocus.in/media/stop-look-wonder/

Ankita Rajasekharan is an educator and nature enthusiast. She enjoys working with children on nature and art based explorations, spending time in the natural environment, observing and documenting small happenings in nature. She has been working as an educator for over 10 years now. She also writes from time to time at: https://earthlingideas.substack.com. She can be reached at 27.ankita@gmail.com.

Shikha Nain is Program Coordinator – Quality at Earth Focus Foundation, develops context-based curricula, leads teacher training, and integrates local knowledge with environmental awareness in rural and tribal communities. Growing up in Haryana and later in Chandigarh, her love for nature, bird watching, and mountains has shaped her belief in inspiring children to connect with and respect their natural environment. She can be reached at shikha@earthfocus.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ankita Rajasekharan and Shikha Nain
From soil painting to mapping forests, Ankita Rajasekharan and Shikha Nain write about a curriculum that turns everyday experiences into moments of learning and wonder.

item.title: A silent guide behind every degree
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Mahima Samanth

A society without teachers is like a body without a heart. This is not just a poetic expression — it is the profound truth. A doctor saves lives, an engineer builds technology, and a scientist invents. But who gave these people the knowledge, the values, and the vision to do what they are doing? The answer: a teacher. Today, the reverence for teachers is gradually fading. In this era of relentless competition, students chase ranks, designations, and salaries. But in the celebration of success, the ones who shaped them  — the teachers — are often forgotten. Even award-winning students rarely mention their mentors. It’s time we reflect on this unfortunate reality. As society sprints behind money and fame, very few see teaching as their dream profession. If everyone desires only power and prestige, who will inspire and guide the next generation of engineers, doctors, scientists, and leaders? Once, children were taught to say, “The farmer feeds us, the teacher enlightens us.” Today, career choices are guided by questions like, “What’s the salary?”, “Are there foreign placements?” But the sacrifice, compassion, and dedication of teachers cannot be measured in currency. The word guru is sacred, but the respect given to this role is diminishing — a worrying sign for any nation. Teaching, once considered a noble calling, is now often viewed as a backup option. If this mindset continues, we may end up with classrooms where machines teach concepts but fail to instil empathy, discipline, or humanity.

Let me share a personal story. I had always been among the top performers — from LKG through BSc, I consistently scored distinctions. But during my final BSc exams, COVID-related delays clashed with the beginning of my MSc classes. Though I had secured the admission, I focused on BSc exams and could not attend online MSc lectures attentively. As a result, I performed poorly in my first internal MSc test. For the first time in my life, I scored below expectations. I felt broken, even considered dropping out. I confided in the Head of my department. She invited me to talk after class and gave me nearly two hours of her time. She listened patiently and then told me, “One poor result doesn’t define your journey, you are capable of far more.” Her words lit a new flame of hope in me. I resumed studying with renewed focus and performed well in the subsequent tests. That day, she didn’t just teach me a lesson, she taught me how to rise again after falling. Teachers are the silent architects of society. They build futures, not just timetables. They deserve high salaries, dignity, job security, and emotional support.

We must teach students to respect their teachers not only when grades or recommendations are needed. On Teacher’s Day or on their birthdays, many students don’t even send a message to those who shaped them. But when fear or confusion strikes, they return to these very teachers. Sadly, some students go so far as to plead excessively or flatter teachers for marks, forgetting that genuine respect cannot be substituted with desperation. Even when they smile, teachers often carry silent burdens. Their strength lies in treating students as their own children, in praying silently for their success, and standing by them in their lowest moments. A teacher is not just someone who explains a concept — they are people who lift us when we fall. They sacrifice silently, expecting nothing in return. Their greatest joy is in seeing us grow. Let us honour them — not with garlands, but with genuine gratitude. If we have grown in life, it is because of their unseen blessings and quiet strength. They are the invisible torchbearers of every success story. Without their light, even the brightest future dims.

Mahima Samanth is a professor in an engineering college with a background in Chemistry. She is passionate about teaching and enjoys writing about values, reflections, and the unseen human side of education. She can be reached at mahimasamanth20@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mahima Samanth
Teaching as a profession no longer holds the same respect and regard that it once did. It is strange though because this is the one profession that instils in students the seeds that will grow to make them doctors, engineers, authors, etc. It is time that we start respecting the guru again.

item.title: Where every child is heard, every child is seen: the role of the school principal in transformational leadership
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Vijay Lakshmi

Every morning, as the school assembly disperses, I make it a point to walk between the rows of students. I place a hand on a shoulder, ask softly, “How are your studies going? Is everything fine at home?” In those few seconds, a child who otherwise may feel invisible suddenly feels acknowledged. These small gestures allow me to interact with hundreds of students each day, reminding them that their voices matter.

Being a principal is more than managing timetables and discipline—it is about ensuring that no child slips through unnoticed. Transformational leadership in schools means creating an environment where every child is heard, every child is seen, and every child is valued.

The principal as a vision-setter

The culture of a school mirrors the values of its leader. When principals set the tone for openness and empathy, teachers and students follow suit. Transformational leadership requires moving away from a rigid administrator’s role to that of a mentor and visionary.

A school is not successful because of its buildings or exam results; it is successful when its students feel safe to express themselves, confident to explore their talents, and motivated to contribute. Leadership, therefore, is about shaping the environment—so that children know that their ideas and efforts matter.

Every child must be heard

Children have ideas, concerns, and dreams—but they need safe platforms to share them. Some of the most powerful changes in my school came from simple interactions:

  • Morning classroom rounds: As I enter classes, I begin with a smile and a question—“How is everyone today?” Applauding good attendance, motivating students with a few encouraging words—these moments build trust.
  • Sessions with monitors and head girls: By empowering student leaders to manage discipline and represent peers, I ensure students learn ownership and responsibility.
  • Morning assembly platforms: Encouraging children to share poems, thoughts, or stories in assembly gives them the confidence to step into the spotlight.

When students realize their principal is not distant but accessible, they begin to speak up. Listening is at the heart of leadership.

Every child must be seen

Recognition is more than announcing toppers’ names. In my school, we celebrate students who show punctuality, kindness, creativity, or leadership. A small innovation we introduced—the Talent Wall—displays artwork, poems, achievements, and creative projects by students across grades. Even a shy student who sketches quietly at home gets the joy of being seen by the entire school community.

This visibility builds self-esteem. A child who is noticed learns to notice others too—creating a cycle of empathy and respect.

Leadership that brings innovation

True leadership is about creating conditions where teachers feel empowered to innovate and students are excited to learn.

One initiative I am especially proud of is the “Lab in a Bag.” Many times, students in classes 6, 7, and 8 are unable to access the science laboratory because of timetable pressures or large class sizes. To solve this, our teachers designed subject-specific “lab bags,” each filled with simple, age-appropriate experiment kits. Now, instead of students always going to the lab, the lab comes to them.

When a teacher walks into class carrying the bag, there is instant excitement. Children pass around materials, perform small experiments at their desks, and suddenly concepts come alive. A lesson on acids and bases, for instance, transforms from a dry definition into a colorful, hands-on experience.

For me, this is the essence of transformational leadership: ensuring that innovation fills the gaps where resources fall short and that no child is denied the joy of learning.

One powerful example I recall is that of a hesitant girl who rarely spoke in class. We encouraged her to recite a poem during one morning assembly. Her voice trembled at first, but the applause she received transformed her. Today, she confidently anchors school programs. That is the power of creating an environment where every child is heard and every child is seen—whether through a platform during assembly or a simple experiment brought to her desk in a bag.

The challenges we face

Large classrooms, exam pressure, limited resources—these are real challenges. Yet, even in such constraints, a principal’s responsibility is to create spaces where children feel valued. Small steps—like regular rounds, personal interactions, or recognition boards—cost nothing but can change everything.

Together with teachers and parents

Transformational leadership cannot rest on the shoulders of the principal alone. Teachers are frontline leaders, and parents are vital partners. By organizing workshops, feedback sessions, and joint projects, we align all stakeholders with the vision of inclusive, child-centered education.

When leadership fosters collaboration, the ecosystem around the child strengthens, ensuring that growth is not just academic but holistic.

Conclusion

Education is not about producing ranks; it is about shaping lives. A school becomes transformational when principals commit to creating an environment where every child is heard and every child is seen.

For me, leadership is not about standing on the stage—it is about walking among my students, asking if they are well, applauding their small victories, empowering them to lead, and celebrating their unique talents. Because when a child feels visible and valued, the school becomes not just a place of study—but a place where life truly begins.

Vijay Lakshmi is a Principal running a senior secondary government school under the Directorate of Education, Government of NCT Delhi. She began her career as lecturer and has 21 years of teaching and learning experience. She can be reached at vijaylakshmik@rediffmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vijay Lakshmi
When we think of a school principal, we think of someone that is stern-looking, distant, and important. But how wrong a picture this is. Principals of schools today understand that true leadership is not being on the stage, but being amidst your students and teachers.

item.title: How India is democratizing AI education and what holds it back
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Shruti Sharma

AI is rewriting the norms. It hasn’t left any sector untouched. India is projected to require approximately one million AI professionals by 2026 to meet industry demand and research needs. In 2024-25, nearly 7,90,999 secondary level students from 4,538 CBSE schools and 50,343 senior secondary students from 944 schools opted for AI as a subject. AI in classrooms is no longer a distant future, it is already unfolding. Yet, democratizing AI education is no small feat, significant challenges remain to implementing AI literacy at scale.

Different policies, programmes, and investments are running parallel under the Indian government to create an AI-ready education system. Let’s take the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, for example, which mandates the integration of 21st century skills including AI in the curricula. The government has allocated ₹500 crore in the Union Budget for FY 2025-26 to institutionalize a Centre of Excellence in AI for Education, focusing on personalized learning, skilling, and workforce readiness. At the state level, Telangana has launched Shiksha Copilot in partnership with Microsoft Research India and Sikshana Foundation to provide personalized lesson plans for heterogeneous classrooms.

Apart from the governments, not-for-profit and philanthropic actors are also actively working to bring AI education to schools. The Central Square Foundation’s AI Samarth, in partnership with the Wadhwani School of Data Science & AI at IIT Madras, is building a comprehensive AI literacy framework that aims to reach 5 million students, parents, and educators within 3–4 years.

It is safe to say that India has moved beyond asking, “Should we teach AI in schools?” The bigger question is, “How do we remove barriers to an AI-literate nation?” The answer lies in confronting four pressing challenges that continue to hold back the vision of AI-ready classrooms.

1. Access to devices and connectivity

Digital access is the foundation for AI education. But significant gaps persist. Only 57.2% of schools have functional computers, and 53.9% have internet access. At the household level, over 95% of Indian households have a telephone or mobile phone, but just 9.9% own a computer. In households with digital access, devices are often shared among family members, limiting their availability for students. Furthermore, ASER’s 2024 report indicates that only 57% students use smartphones for educational purposes. This makes scaling AI literacy a highly uneven journey for students from low-income backgrounds.

2. Inclusive and holistic curriculum

AI is being rolled out in stages, but gaps remain. CBSE offers AI as a skill subject from class IX onwards, with a 15-hour foundational module available from class VI in some schools. Technical depth is confined mainly to secondary and higher secondary levels, while younger students receive only a conceptual introduction. There is no universally adopted, all-grades curriculum with consistent technical rigour across school boards or states. In contrast, Western models often integrate age-appropriate AI exposure from primary to senior levels. India’s fragmented approach risks creating unequal readiness.

3. Student data and privacy

AI literacy is about responsible use. AI in schools could expose children to risks of data misuse and surveillance without proper data security measures. Karnataka’s plan to use AI-based face recognition for school attendance triggered alarms about student privacy. Education apps like DIKSHA have previously exposed sensitive teacher and student data due to server misconfigurations. Schools need robust mechanisms to protect student data and prevent misuse while integrating AI in curriculums.

4. AI’s impact on critical thinking

Educators must promote the idea of responsible AI usage among students. Emerging research indicates that excessive AI usage can adversely impact students' higher-order thinking skills when used as a substitute for critical thinking. Students must be made aware of both the pros and cons of using AI. The AI curriculum should provide students with the tools to identify ethical and right use of AI with real-life applications.

India is making meaningful strides toward preparing students for an AI-enabled world. Yet, oversights remain: patchy device access, inadequate privacy safeguards, and fragmented curricula could leave many students behind. If these gaps can be addressed proactively, AI literacy will become widespread and equitable. The next generation of school-goers will then not only be AI-ready when they enter the workforce but also capable of shaping AI responsibly and inclusively.

Shruti Sharma is a public policy student at Takshashila Institution and a former Teach For India Fellow. Over the past three years, she has worked across classrooms and data operations, exploring how education and policy can come together to create meaningful change. She can be reached atShruti100sharma10@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shruti Sharma
AI is the future and reconginzing its importance the Indian government and a few other actors, including NGOs, are launching programmes to get our students, the citizens of tomorrow, ready for an AI world. But if we do not want to fall behind other countries, we have to overcome a few challenges.

item.title: Single most important learning
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

In his Ted Talk Alan Watkins asks this question: “If you could teach yourself, your children, anybody – just ONE thing, what would that one thing be?”

In an era that is overflowing with information – often available at a single click – I found this to be a powerful question indeed. As teachers, we are most often weighed down by heavy syllabi and packed timetables. Sifting through this morass for ONE most important learning seems impossible – or does it?

An online community of teachers didn’t think it was impossible. In fact, they found it worthwhile to ruminate on. In the three-minute clip (link at the end of this article), each one articulated what they regarded as the single most important learning.

The need for self-reflection was seen as the topmost priority for one teacher. I was struck by the fact that she emphasized ‘the need’ rather than just ‘self-reflection’. For, unless one is convinced of the need for such a practice, why would one indulge in it regularly? Of course, that led me to wonder exactly how she would get her students to experience the criticality of this need …. Another teacher named a related trait, viz., self-regulation as her single most important learning.

Adaptability was seen as crucial by another teacher: the importance of remaining pliable to the ever-changing demands of the world. I could see the importance of this, given the fast pace at which everything changes in today’s world. “To pause and observe little things,” declared another teacher. This made me stop in my tracks! I immediately paused and asked myself: “How often do I do that?” I concluded that I hadn’t really learnt to do this.

In fact, this led me to ask: How could adaptability be reconciled with pausing to notice little things? (Could it?)

To live in harmony with the world was seen as the next teacher’s single most important lesson. Quite a tall order! Could this really be called a SINGLE most important learning? Surely this must encompass numerous learnings under such a broad umbrella!

I am sure many readers resonate with the next two: Getting her students to simply enjoy the process of learning was seen as the most important thing for another teacher. Awakening a love for reading was yet another teacher’s single most important learning for her students. Who wouldn’t agree with these two?

Listening skills were highlighted by a teacher as being her most important teaching. Simple as this sounds, haven’t we all experienced not being listened to? What does it take to listen to another with full attention? And how often do we do it ourselves?

Compassion for oneself and others was deemed a very important learning by one teacher, while another stressed the importance of empathy. So also did a teacher value the trait of trusting oneself and others.

Working with one’s hands was regarded by one teacher as being her most important lesson. I couldn’t help wondering: (how) can this element be brought into every single subject?

Being mindful in the here and now was a teacher’s high-priority lesson. Gratitude was topmost on another teacher’s checklist.

Respecting multiple viewpoints was raised as a very important learning by a teacher.

All of this left me asking: if these are teachers’ single most important learnings, when (if ever) are we going to get around to teaching these things? WHY aren’t we doing so already? Clearly, teachers are not at liberty to teach what they deem to be most important, and instead, are compelled to follow the diktats of policy makers and textbook writers.

If you watch this clip here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x2EwHL9hn3s

Perhaps you will begin articulating your single most important learning too?

And then, maybe, even draw up your road map for actually bringing that lesson into your classroom?

Neeraja Raghavan is Founder-Director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
What is the single most important learning you would want to teach yourself, your children, or your students? Neeraja Raghavan shares how different teachers responded to this question.

item.title: Exploring code-switching in Indian classrooms
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Bharti Tyagi

Language is the soul of learning; it is through language that we name the world, frame our experiences, and enter into dialogue with others. In classrooms, it becomes the medium through which knowledge is both constructed and evaluated. The classroom is also the place where we learn languages that home doesn’t teach us. English is one of them for most Indian children. English occupies an uneasy space, visible on school notice boards and report cards, in teachers’ words, and yet largely absent from the streets, homes, and the general ethos. While English fluency is institutionally upheld as a marker of academic and social mobility, it remains largely disconnected from the linguistic reality that surrounds an average student. I started noticing this very early in the first year of my experience as an English teacher in the city of Haridwar. The texture of daily life was woven with Hindi and speech varieties spoken in Uttarakhand, but the efficacy of schools was measured on the criteria of their ability to make children fluent in English. Kumar (1996) critically examines this paradox, arguing that the dominance of English in Indian schooling reflects a postcolonial legacy where language becomes a site of cultural alienation. He contends that the imposition of English often undermines the child’s experiential world, creating a disconnect between home and school. In such a system, language is not merely a tool of learning but a symbol of power and exclusion, where the vernacular is subtly devalued and English is upheld as the language of success, thereby reinforcing existing social hierarchies.

This tension was an everyday reality of my classroom, where students, rooted in Hindi-speaking households, were expected to read, write, and speak fluently in English. It all started when I noticed grades 6-8 children mixing up similar sounding words which were not homophones and were completely unrelated to the context in which they were being used. Some of the mix-ups were funny as the more fluent children would immediately point to the error and the speaker would find herself in a ‘spot’ (Holt, 1977) and feel humiliated. For instance, ‘shoulders’ for ‘soldiers’, ‘electrical’ for ‘electoral’ (politics), ‘corporation’ for ‘cooperation’, ‘there’ for ‘their’, ‘principal’ for ‘principle’, ‘weather’ for ‘whether’, and so on. Such instances forced me to pay a greater attention because I realized soon that they were not really pronunciation errors or errors borne out of carelessness. They encapsulated a struggle of learning a language that is not part of the larger socio-cultural ethos.

This article is a reflection of the observations I made and the challenges I encountered while teaching English. Each episode I mention here is both a window into a specific classroom moment and a doorway into broader issues that deserve our attention.

Episode 1: “Ma’am, I know the answer… par English mein nahi aata!”

It was a Tuesday morning. We were working on adjectives using a visual cue activity, a method I often rely on to bridge conceptual understanding and language use. That day, I projected an image of a dog leaping over a fence and asked the students to describe it using three adjectives. Aarav, a quiet but perceptive boy, raised his hand, his face lighting up. “Ma’am, mujhe answer aata hai!” (Ma’am, I know the answer!), he exclaimed. But the moment I invited him to speak, his body language changed. His voice dropped. His confidence deflated. “Par English mein nahi aata,” (But I don’t know how to articulate the answer in English), he whispered, almost apologetically.

That sentence lodged itself in my memory, not because he didn’t know the answer, but because he did. His mind was clear. His understanding sharp. He was ready with “chanchal, bhagtahua, sundar,” (playful, running, beautiful), but English had become a gatekeeper. In that instant, the very language we honour as a means of accessing opportunities had turned into a wall. Like many of his peers, this boy could navigate classroom routines in English, requesting a pencil or responding to yes/no questions, but academic discourse, which demands structured expression, nuanced vocabulary, and grammatical precision, placed him in a state of linguistic paralysis. This is not a failure of knowledge but a crisis of expression, one born from the burden of mentally translating conceptual clarity in Hindi into ‘acceptable’ English.

In retrospect, this incident also exposed the limitations of monolingual classroom ideologies, especially in the context of a private school, which differentiates itself for its claim of teaching English. These institutions, often striving to brand themselves as ‘English-medium,’ create environments where the use of Hindi, even as a scaffold, is subtly discouraged, if not outright penalized. The institutional emphasis on ‘English only’ policies, often motivated by parents’ demand and the promise of upward social mobility, ironically leads to a culture where students rehearse memorized phrases, devoid of comprehension or context. Many end up producing what can only be described as ‘broken fluency’, grammatical constructions repeated by heart, but stripped of voice, intent, and agency.

This moment also brought to mind the translanguaging approach developed by García and Wei (2014), which argues that bilingual students should be allowed to mobilize their entire linguistic repertoire, Hindi, English, and everything in between, to learn meaningfully. If I had allowed Aarav to express his response first in Hindi and then guided him through collaborative translation with the class, he might not only have spoken but also felt seen and affirmed. Translanguaging does not dilute English acquisition; it strengthens it by making the learning process cognitively accessible and emotionally resonant.

And yet, in many private schools, translanguaging remains almost impossible to implement authentically. The classroom functions like a ‘black box’, a sealed linguistic domain where only English is permitted entry. Hindi, the language in which children think, reason, and dream, is rendered invisible. As teachers, we are often caught in a pedagogical paradox: we are expected to teach English meaningfully, but denied access to the very tools – students' home languages – that would make such learning meaningful.

Aarav’s moment of hesitation was not a lack of ability, but a reflection of how language policies and classroom cultures conspire to marginalize knowledge that lives in the ‘wrong’ language. His silence was a form of resistance, a quiet plea to be allowed to think and speak bilingually.

Episode 2: “Grammar is like maths, no?”

During a lesson on the present perfect tense, I noticed a student scribbling equations in the margin of her notebook. Curious, I asked her what she was doing. She smiled and said, “Ma’am, grammar is like maths, na? Bas rule yaad rakho: has/have + third form.” (Ma’am, grammar is like maths, right? Just remember the rule: has/have + third form).

Her interpretation wasn’t wrong, but it was revealing. She craved structure in the chaos of English. She was trying to ‘solve’ the language like an equation. But when asked to write a paragraph about her weekend using the tense, she faltered. The formula helped her form sentences, but not meaning. An odd similarity that often surfaces in English classrooms in Hindi-speaking areas like Haridwar is that pupils confuse grammar with mathematics. This contrast, despite its apparent innocuousness, reveals a deeper cognitive pattern influenced by the formal educational system. Many students are made to believe that grammar is like maths, rule-bound, systematic, and outcome-driven. During tense drills, transformation exercises, and sentence construction activities, this parallel frequently comes up. The students enquire, "Ma'am, rule kya hai?" (Ma'am, what is the rule?), looking for a universally applicable prescriptive framework.

This perception is neither incidental nor entirely incorrect. It emerges from the way English grammar is taught in many Indian classrooms: through rule memorization, worksheets, and formulaic correctness. However, this method foregrounds syntactic accuracy while marginalizing semantic appropriateness and communicative intent. Students may become adept at filling in blanks or converting direct to indirect speech but falter when asked to compose a meaningful paragraph or engage in extended discourse. Krashen (1982) encapsulated this tension in his critique of form-focused training. According to Krashen, learning a language through explicit grammar teaching is rarely successful unless it is combined with comprehensible input, language that is just a little bit above the learner's current proficiency level but is made clear through conversation, gestures, context, and visuals. When meaning and grammar are taught separately, students may internalize rules but not use them in authentic communication situations.

The problem is not just pedagogical, it is institutional. English language instruction in most private, government, and government-aided schools in India tends to prioritize surface-level correctness over depth of understanding. Assignments are marked for punctuation and verb forms, but rarely for nuance, coherence, or audience awareness. This results in a mechanical use of English, devoid of agency or contextual sensitivity. This ‘grammar-is-maths’ analogy reveals the limited ways in which language is presented in Hindi-medium and English-medium classrooms alike. Students are rarely invited to see grammar as dynamic, fluid, and context-sensitive. Unless grammar is embedded within discourse, supported through interaction, and freed from the tyranny of error-counting, students will continue to write like technicians rather than thinkers.

Episode 3: “Speaking English is showing off.”

One afternoon, while on substitution duty during recess, I overheard a group of students teasing a classmate who had just responded to a teacher in fluent English. “Zyada English mat show off kar, yeh Haridwar hai, London nahi,” (Don't show off your English, this is Haridwar, not London), one boy quipped. When the others started laughing, the boy, who was clearly humiliated, left the conversation and turned to go. Later on, I started to wonder if the experience had affected him in any way. Even though he was competent in English, had he grown to feel that being able to speak the language was something to be laughed at rather than valued?

This episode revealed a crucial yet often ignored sociolinguistic barrier in English language classrooms: the tension between linguistic competence and social acceptability. In schools located in semi-urban Hindi-speaking regions like Haridwar, English is often simultaneously coveted and resented. While it is associated with aspiration and opportunity, it is also perceived by some as a marker of elitism, especially when fluency is unevenly distributed across the classroom. Students who speak confidently in English may be labelled as “show-offs” by their peers, creating an environment where linguistic participation is socially risky.

This dynamic can be understood through Bourdieu’s (1991) concept of linguistic capital. English, in this context, functions as a privileged code, one that carries symbolic power and aligns with institutional legitimacy. Yet, for many students, especially those whose home environments do not reinforce English use, this symbolic capital becomes a site of alienation. They either resist acquiring it or, worse, acquire it silently without daring to use it publicly. Fluency, then, is not just a pedagogical outcome but a socially negotiated identity.

Norton's (2000) theory of language investment provides additional insight by highlighting the fact that language learners are active participants whose learning is connected to their social identity and future aspirations rather than passive recipients. Because of emotional and social disinvestment more than a lack of ability, learners become less motivated to learn a language if speaking it jeopardises their sense of peer belonging or exposes them to mockery. This tension is particularly pronounced in private and government-aided schools that promote English as a status symbol but do little to cultivate a supportive linguistic culture. ‘English-speaking’ becomes a performative label, not a functional skill. Teachers are often caught between enforcing English-medium policies and managing classroom dynamics that penalize students who do speak it fluently.

Pedagogy beyond compliance

The task of teaching English in Hindi-dominant social spaces such as Haridwar demands more than linguistic training; it demands a reorientation of how we imagine the role of English within the ecology of a multilingual education. Rather than treating language learning as a ladder to be climbed, educators and institutions must begin to view classrooms as linguistic environments, where each language plays an interdependent and generative role. English, in such a view, is not a replacement language but a relational one, one that coexists with, and is often made possible through, the learner’s first language.

This shift requires teachers to work not only at the level of method but at the level of belief. A classroom that merely tolerates home languages during comprehension but insists on their erasure in production remains locked in a deficit model of bilingualism. What is needed is a pedagogy of reciprocity, where meaning-making becomes a collaborative process rather than a hierarchical display of correctness. It is within such pedagogical relationships that students develop what Canagarajah (2013) terms ‘negotiation strategies’, the ability to mediate between languages, audiences, and expectations in contextually intelligent ways.

Equally important is the need to reconsider how teacher education programs in India prepare future English language teachers. While most training continues to emphasize structuralist grammars, pronunciation drills, and British or American models of correctness, little space is given to exploring the sociolinguistic realities of Indian classrooms, where code-switching is not a flaw but a norm. Addressing this gap involves reimagining teacher preparation itself, not as technical training but as critical language awareness (Fairclough, 1992), where teachers learn to interrogate the power structures embedded in language use and policy.

References

  • Bourdieu, P. (1991). Language and symbolic power (J. B. Thompson, Ed.; G. Raymond & M. Adamson, Trans.). Harvard University Press.
  • Canagarajah, S. (2013). Translingual practice: Global Englishes and cosmopolitan relations. Routledge.
  • Fairclough, N. (1992). Critical language awareness. Longman.
  • García, O., & Wei, L. (2014). Translanguaging: Language, bilingualism and education. Palgrave Macmillan.
  • Holt, J. (1977). How Children Learn. Harmondsworth: Penguin Books.
  • Krashen, S. D. (1982). Principles and practice in second language acquisition. Pergamon Press.
  • Kumar, K. (1996). Two Worlds in Learning from Conflict. Orient BlackSwan.
  • Norton, B. (2000). Identity and language learning: Gender, ethnicity and educational change. Longman.

Bharti Tyagi teaches English at M.C.S. Bal Vidyapeeth, Haridwar, Uttarakhand. She recently completed her Master’s in Education from the Department of Education (CIE), University of Delhi. Her interests include philosophy and sociology of education, language education, and modernity-tradition dynamics in Indian schooling. She can be reached at bhartityagi04.bt@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Bharti Tyagi
Teaching English in Hindi-speaking regions reveals paradoxes. Bharti Tyagi advocates for students’ home languages in the classroom.

item.title: From awareness to action: schools as catalysts for gender sensitivity and inclusion
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aprajita Ralli

“Schools are not just mirrors of society – they are its architects.”

At Sreenidhi International School, gender sensitivity is not an occasional campaign. It is part of our curriculum, teaching, and culture. Beyond academics, we are committed to raising young people who are empathetic, socially responsible, and courageous enough to question inequality.

When curriculum becomes advocacy
