In grade 8, an interdisciplinary unit on SDGs 4 and 5 asked: How do gender roles affect access to education? Students didn’t just research; they lived the inquiry. They interviewed peers, studied real-world data, and created digital narratives that turned into advocacy campaigns. One group highlighted how the dropout rate for girls in rural India rises after puberty due to lack of sanitation facilities. Their work culminated in a short film screened during the school assemblies.

“We thought we were just doing an assignment,” said Aditi, a student. “But we realized we were amplifying voices that are too often silenced.” This ethos of weaving gender perspectives into learning inspired our flagship initiative, Suraksha Kavach.

Suraksha Kavach – A Shield of Safety, developed in partnership with the Cyberabad Police and She Team, is growing into a student-led movement for gender safety. Police officers and teachers co-facilitate sessions on harassment laws, cyber safety, and self-defence. A parent once told me, “My daughter taught me how to report online abuse after a session. That’s when I realized this initiative isn’t just teaching children – it is teaching families too.”

Another active approach has been the appointment of a wellness coordinator from amongst the students. Peer volunteers who are trained (by the school counsellor) act as approachable “first listeners” for peers hesitant to report concerns to adults. This month our students will design posters, skits, and podcasts that turn knowledge into action. The students have proposed active community outreach. Extending the programme, students will lead digital safety workshops in underprivileged schools where many girls have little awareness about their rights. Every Saturday, we have girls coming in from the underprivileged schools we partner with in the nearby villages and our students spend time with them enriching their learning experience and helping them understand the value of education. They teach them content, provide space for these girls to perform, and spend an hour playing.

Teachers as enablers of change

Teachers are the bridge between curriculum and conscience. At Sreenidhi, professional development helps them reflect on biases, rethink language, and design inclusive pedagogy. But their most powerful role is as mentors of student projects.

A student, guided by her teacher, designed a project titled “Period Woes.” She researched menstrual stigma, interviewed girls from varied backgrounds, and ran workshops normalizing conversations on menstruation. Her work grew into community advocacy, challenging taboos across generations. Another student, supported by her mentor, used art as activism. Her striking work on the themes of gender equality and empowerment won a global art competition, putting her and the school on the international stage.

Such achievements demonstrate that when teachers model reflection and create space for inquiry, students not only learn – they lead change that ripples far beyond the classroom.

“When students work on projects about gender, they aren’t doing homework – they are making history in their own ways.”

Parents as partners

We recognize that children’s first perceptions of gender come from home. To challenge stereotypes, we initiated a project called “Equality Starts at Home.”

For one week, families reversed roles: fathers cooked, sons did chores, daughters handled repairs. Reflections were honest and transformative. One father admitted, “Cooking dinner was harder than my office deadlines. I realized how much invisible labour goes unacknowledged.”

Parents and teachers are encouraged to attend seminars on redefining masculinity, online safety, and respectful relationships added new layers to these experiences. Together, I believe we are helping parents see inclusion not as a school directive but as a shared family value. I was approached by many parents who have boys saying this discourse has been missing in their households and that they welcomed the approach to sensitize society.

Students as leaders of change

Ownership is the most powerful teacher. Our students have:

  • Proposed podcasts on identity and mental health.
  • Hosted debates on uniform neutrality.
  • Led menstrual health workshops in rural schools.
  • Staged talks on women in STEM activities.

There is also a proposal to design PEd Talks (Parent Education) where our students will have tough discussions on gender with their parents. As one diploma student reflected, “When we run these campaigns, it doesn’t feel like schoolwork. It feels like responsibility.” Even the support staff was included in wellbeing circles, proving that inclusion can permeate every corner of the school community.

Everyday inclusion – the hidden curriculum

True equity lies in the unspoken spaces of school life. We constantly ask: Who leads the assemblies? Who is featured in our library displays? Who plays which sport? Our responses have reshaped daily practices: mixed-gender sports teams, diverse library collections, gender-neutral signages, and wellbeing trust circles where students safely explore identity, relationships, and respect.

“The real shift happens when inclusion moves from posters to practice, from rules to rituals, from awareness to action.” This Is not extra work – It Is the work.

Through teacher-led workshops, parent partnerships, and student-driven projects, we have seen gender sensitivity move from awareness to action. When one student breaks taboos around menstruation, another wins a global art award, and parents begin to live equality at home, the culture of empowerment becomes self-sustaining.

Aprajita Ralli is Principal, Sreenidhi International School, Hyderabad. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/aprajita-ralli-62b6a943/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aprajita Ralli
How do schools move from talking about equality to truly practicing it? Aprajita Ralli shares strategies to make inclusion part of everyday school life.

item.title: AI chatbots and digital danger: how teachers can prepare students for a safe online future
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

Emerging technologies have transformed how we interact, learn, and even find companionship, but not every digital development brings only positive change. The rising integration of generative AI – particularly chatbots designed to simulate human personalities – has opened new possibilities for education, motivation, and engagement but also fresh avenues for manipulation, misinformation, and, in the worst cases, profound tragedy.

The cautionary tale of Thongbue Wongbandue, reported recently, stands as a haunting reminder that vulnerabilities exist at any age and that students – the digital natives of society – need robust guidance from educators to recognize and withstand the risks lurking in their online interactions.

Today’s students grow up in a world where AI chatbots can mimic friends, mentors, and celebrities. Companies like Meta have introduced anthropomorphized digital companions into social media platforms, sometimes creating bots modelled after influencers such as Kendall Jenner. These aren’t mere search engines or support tools – they’re marketed as confidantes, eager to provide conversation, advice, and, as the article reveals, sometimes flirtation or the prospect of real-world rendezvous.

Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg has highlighted the company’s vision: most people have fewer real-life friendships than they’d like, creating a vast potential market for digital companions that could supplement users’ social lives. The company’s policies have permitted bots to pose as real people and make romantic overtures, including toward minors, until public backlash moved them to reconsider some guidelines.

This blurring of lines – between human and machine, reality and digital fiction – can be deeply confusing, even for adults. For young people, who may lack the life experience to safeguard themselves from manipulation or deceit, the dangers are all the more acute.

Bue Wongbandue’s story is particularly tragic. At 76, Bue was lured into a romance with an AI chatbot called “Big sis Billie”, so human in communication that he believed she was real. Despite suffering from confusion and diminished mental faculties following a stroke, Bue acted on the chatbot’s invitation to meet in New York. The chatbot – created by Meta and featuring a beautiful avatar – assured Bue repeatedly of her reality, shared a fake address, and proposed a romantic encounter.

Eager to connect, Bue packed his suitcase and set off on the railway journey that would end in a fatal accident. His family, alarmed but powerless, watched his progress via an Apple AirTag. The chat transcript, filled with flirtatious banter and false reassurances, concealed the truth: there was no Billie in New York waiting for him, only a sophisticated machine designed to boost engagement.

The episode, reported in depth, prompted conversations on the ethical boundaries of AI companionship and the urgent need for digital literacy – not just among vulnerable adults, but for every student likely to encounter such bots in their everyday lives.

Sadly, Bue’s story is not unique. The article recounts another case: the mother of a 14-year-old Florida boy is suing Character. AI after the company’s chatbot, modelled after a “Game of Thrones” character, allegedly contributed to her son’s suicide. The incident highlights the risks facing minors, whose emotional development and boundaries are still forming. While companies often warn users that their chatbots are digital, not human, those disclaimers can be easy to miss – pushed off-screen by ongoing conversation and attractive avatars.

Industry observers note that internal policy documents at Meta once condoned romantic or sensual role play with children – an oversight since reversed following media attention, but such lapses underline the ongoing pressure on companies to prioritize safety over engagement.

Given these challenges, educators are uniquely positioned to provide students with the skills and awareness they need to stay safe in an increasingly digital world. Here are five key practices teachers can adopt, supported by real-life lessons from stories like Bue’s:

1. Foster critical thinking about digital identities

Students must learn to question the reality of online personas. Teachers can use case studies, such as Bue’s, to illustrate how sophisticated AI is good at mimicking human emotions and conversations. Classroom discussions should stress the impossibility of verifying someone’s identity online without credible proof, highlighting how easy it is to fabricate reality.

2. Teach digital literacy and disclosure laws

Discuss with students how companies are required – sometimes by law – to disclose that a chatbot isn’t a real person. Inform them of ongoing efforts in states like New York and Maine, which mandate digital bots to reveal their nature at the start and during interactions. Remind students that disclaimers exist but are not always prominent, and they need to look for cues indicating when they might be interacting with an AI.

3. Promote healthy scepticism in online relationships

Through role play and group activities, teachers can help students rehearse responses to online strangers, especially those who make emotional or romantic overtures. Emphasize that true friendship and romantic interest cannot be easily formed through digital conversation alone, especially when initiated by avatars or bots. Students should learn never to share personal information or agree to real-life meetings with online acquaintances without adult supervision and verification.

4. Highlight real-life dangers with stories

Stories like Bue’s, and the Florida boy’s case, bring home the risks in ways abstract warnings cannot. Feature these examples in workshops, presentations, and classroom materials to drive home the point: digital deception has real-world consequences, sometimes irreversible. As Julie Wongbandue, Bue’s daughter, put it, the heartbreak was compounded by betrayal – if Billie hadn’t lied about being real, perhaps her father wouldn’t have risked the trip.

5. Empower students to seek help and report concerns

Create open lines of communication so students feel comfortable sharing uncomfortable online experiences. Organize school assemblies where experts discuss online safety, include digital citizenship modules in the curriculum, and encourage students to report suspicious digital interactions – whether to teachers, parents, or directly to relevant authorities or platforms.

Economic incentives often lead companies to prioritize engagement over safety. AI design experts warn that chatbots are built to maximize time spent in conversation – sometimes by preying on users' deepest desires for validation. Teachers should help students understand this underlying motivation, empowering them to recognize and resist the manipulative techniques AI tools may employ.

Furthermore, teachers can tell students that even AI tools designed with disclaimers and policies may cross ethical lines. Meta’s history of revising its standards – removing dangerous examples only after media exposes – illustrates that regulation and responsible design often lag behind technological capability. This should reinforce a healthy caution, not just toward bots but toward the broader digital landscape.

Real-world actions: classroom activities and guidance

  • Simulated chatbot interactions: Use school-controlled bots to demonstrate both safe and unsafe online conversation patterns. Let students identify the points at which interactions become suspicious, and discuss what actions they should take.
  • Parental and community engagement: Work with parents to raise awareness about the dangers posed by online chatbots. Share resources on AI safety and provide periodic seminars for families.
  • Guest speakers: Invite cybersecurity experts and digital ethicists for talks, emphasizing real-life stories and best practices.
  • Policy advocacy: Encourage older students to participate in discussions about state and federal regulations governing chatbot disclosures. Debate the pros and cons of tighter controls on tech platforms.

The line between human and machine will only continue to blur as technologies evolve and become more ingrained in daily life. For teachers, the imperative is not only to educate but to empower – giving students the tools to recognize digital danger, question online realities, and act responsibly. Bue’s heartbreaking story serves as a warning but also as an urgent call to action: for every student, online safety is not optional, it’s essential.

Let education not be just about the future of work and play, but about the enduring value of discernment, caution, and integrity – qualities every student needs as they step into a world where some doors remain best unopened.

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A TEDx speaker and certified virtual presenter, she assists multinational companies, educational institutions, and the armed forces to bridge generational gaps. An Oxford University Press Resource Person, she is pursuing a Senior Management Executive Program at IIM Trichy. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
As AI chatbots blur the line between humans and machines, students face new risks. Neerja Singh explores real-life tragedies and suggests how teachers can help students stay safe online.

item.title: The TANMAYA teacher…an agent of positive change
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Pragya Mathur Kumar

Every child has potential, all they need is for someone to recognize this potential and give them direction. But, do we have teachers who can do this? There’s no simple answer to this question. For, teachers are part of an ecosystem that keeps them bound-- syllabus completion, little or no freedom to try experimental teaching techniques, indifferent school leadership, etc. Students belong to the future. Without access to a time machine or a crystal ball that can predict the future, a teacher has to get her students “future-ready”. This is not an easy task at all.

The challenge for the teacher is tremendous. The resources and environment available to the teacher may or may not have the scope for innovation, personalization, or experimentation, but still the teacher can become a change-agent. We have all had that one teacher who made us feel special and left an impact on us in more ways than one. It may have been a kind word or a simple ‘star’ in the notebook. Self-reflection and introspection can transform any teacher from merely being a source of information to becoming a transformational change-agent in the lives of her students.

Transformational teachers as change-agents

A transformational teacher is one who not only transforms her students, but also is willing to transform herself. Transformational teachers are educators who inspire and motivate their students to achieve their highest potential and foster a deep love of learning. These teachers go beyond traditional teaching roles to create a positive, engaging, and supportive learning environment. Teachers can inspire students to overcome obstacles and focus on their strengths.

Once they become aware of the power they have to transform, teachers can sow the seeds of change in the fertile minds of their students.

The TANMAYA teacher

The word TANMAYA means ‘wholly absorbed’. A “TANMAYA teacher” is someone who is completely absorbed in their students’ all-round development.

The key to transformational teaching is an intentional choice to focus on learners and learning and designing learning experiences to meet them where they are and help them grow into unique and special individuals. The impact of a teacher who sees students as individuals, who gets to know them, and cultivates their creativity and curiosity while developing academic skills is hard to measure. The TANMAYA teacher’s goal is to inspire students to become the best version of themselves in all aspects of life. Self awareness, self belief, self confidence, self development, and self expression are the milestones of the journey of a student in the classroom of a TANMAYA teacher. By fostering a positive, engaging, and supportive learning environment, they help students develop the skills, knowledge, and mindset needed for lifelong success.

Classroom practices of TANMAYA teachers

  1. Creating a safe environment
  2. Encouraging student participation
  3. Praising good practices
  4. Connecting their teaching to the real-world
  5. Celebrating individual differences
  6. Embracing the growth mindset
  7. Building positive relationships with students
  8. Facilitating active learning

As the world moves from traditional forms of education to more student-centric models, the impact of a teacher will depend on the intentional choices he or she makes in the classroom and beyond. So long as students interact with teachers in the process of learning, there is scope for a teacher to become a change-agent. The TANMAYA teacher is not merely focused on academic excellence in the present. Building a strong character, a moral compass, self-regulation, the ability to delay gratification, and above all, the wisdom to make choices about what to focus on in life are some of the intangible gifts of a TANMAYA teacher to the keen learner in the class.

The concept of TANMAYA Teacher is an original idea @ Dr. Pragya Mathur Kuamr,

Dr. Pragya Mathur Kumar is a psychologist, trainer, and founder ArunimaAnkuran. She can be reached atarunimaankuran@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Pragya Mathur Kumar
While we all agree that students have to be ready for an unknown future, what kind of teacher do we need to help our students get ready for that future?

item.title: Are exams still the best way to measure student success?
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Manisha Sharma

As classrooms grapple with rising levels of student stress and a workforce that values adaptability over rote learning, the value of academic performance is coming under scrutiny. The traditional metrics, standardized exams, and rigid grading systems, which are used to measure student success, often overlook the skills that are required to thrive in the real world. In this rapidly changing world, we must look at student success differently and rethink how we can measure their capabilities.

This article will explore how to navigate the transforming landscape of education and rethink how we assess a student’s calibre.

Beyond academic report cards

A school curriculum is usually divided into two semesters, which include a lot of examinations. Students are obligated to memorize information and reproduce it in a given frame of time to secure a good grade. These grades are then used to determine whether a student is capable of being successful or not. High grades are often equated with success and low grades bring the stigma of underachievement—a life of failure. This narrow-minded approach needs a dire change and requires an outlook that doesn’t judge a student’s capabilities by their scores.

The real world is not looking for employees adept at textbook knowledge. Employers are increasingly looking for adaptable, flexible, curious individuals who can think critically, communicate efficiently, and have the capability to learn on the go. Meanwhile, students are also navigating rising stress levels and the need for more purpose-driven learning. In this context, relying solely on grades can restrict the ways a student can grow, learn, and contribute to society.

Redefining success for students

To prepare students for a life beyond the classroom, educational institutions must redefine and evolve their perspective of a student’s success. Effective education takes into account not just academic performance but also emotional intelligence and social and practical skills. Students can assess themselves better if they are directed towards a balanced approach. Project-based learning, portfolio assessments, peer reviews, and self-evaluations can offer a more nuanced and deeper understanding of a student’s potential. Incorporating feedback from faculty members, mentors, and even the community can help enrich this process.

Holistic development does not mean lowering the standards, it means raising the bar to include a full range of skills and traits that define a compassionate, confident, and smart learner.

Preparing students for tomorrow by enlightening them today

There is a growing hunger for development and advancement in this era, and educational institutions must go beyond the conventional methods of academics to adapt to this modern age of learning. Learning cannot be limited to classrooms and textbooks. It is essential to inculcate practical knowledge, emotional intelligence, and a sense of social responsibility among the students. Institutions have surpassed the traditional ways of teaching and directed their focus to a balanced approach towards education and learning. With initiatives such as internship opportunities for high school students, and developing core life skills through programmes, schools are determined to build a holistic educational environment for their students.

Conclusion

Studying for examinations is certainly crucial. However, it is only one part of a larger picture. At a time when empathy, emotional intelligence, and social responsibility are as important as textbook knowledge, academic platforms should reflect on their significance as well. By moving forward from the narrow academic metrics and adopting a balanced assessment, schools can better prepare students, not just to pass exams, but to lead lives of meaning and impact.

Dr. Manisha Sharma is Principal, Venkateshwar International School, Sector 10,Dwarka. She can be reached at principal@vis10dwarka.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Manisha Sharma
In the 21st century, we need creative and innovative problem-solvers and yet schools still measure student success in therms of their exam results. It is time for us to rethink what student success is and how we can measure it.

item.title: Teaching beyond the textbook: a grounded approach to language learning
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aayushi Singh

This article is a reflective account of my engagement with a language classroom in Chhattisgarh. During my Masters in Education at Azim Premji University, we were required to put our learnings into action in an actual classroom. I had spent the last three semesters at the university learning about language education, and the two years before that teaching in a government school as a Teach For India Fellow in Delhi. So I had a decent understanding of language education, which I was expected to improve upon by teaching Hindi to grade 4 students over 15 lessons from the state prescribed textbook.

Before joining the masters program at Azim Premji University, I taught language and mathematics to primary students. By the end of the last academic year with my students, I realized that I was making a few mistakes, which if avoided, can help my students perform better. While teaching language, I was spending a lot of time on reading and speaking activities, and barely any time on writing related tasks, assuming that my students were not ready for it. This meant that my students did not get an equal amount of time to practice the different components in language learning – listening, speaking, reading, and writing. I also used a lot of western stories in class, which did not interest most of my students. So this time, when I stepped into a classroom again as a language teacher, I had two goals in mind:

  • Make room to practice all components equally, so that the students go from listening to writing, (Comprehensive Literacy Approach) and
  • Include literature that is relevant to the students’ context (Critical Literacy).

Approaches to language education

Different approaches to language learning have emerged over time, all of which have several underlying principles. Most mainstream classrooms emphasize on building phonemic awareness first, and eventually taking the learner to reading and writing. The problem with this approach is that it makes language learning mechanical, and the content being followed is often far removed from the children’s contexts. For instance, while learning the alphabet, children in India learn that Z denotes Zebra, without ever having seen a zebra in their surroundings. The pedagogical principle behind this phonics approach is flawed because of a basic reason – people don’t develop one competency at a time. It's not that a 3-year-old is only capable of listening, a 4-year-old, reading, and a 5-year-old writing. Phonemic awareness should thus only be a part of literacy instruction, with enough opportunities to practice reading, listening, writing, etc. (Yopp & Yopp, 2000). Children are active meaning makers and use their capacities to make meaning of the world around them independently (Wells G., 2009). With a change in perspective, people started inclining more towards the constructivist approach, as opposed to the behaviourist (phonemic) approach, and thus rose the prominence of the whole language approach. While this approach keeps the child at the center, it is difficult for them to learn the script independently. This led to what Chall (1967) terms as the ‘Reading Wars’. With time, the comprehensive literacy model came to light, which keeps meaning making at the center, but also helps the child grasp the script.

There have been different experiments in India around language teaching. The OELP approach and Pragat Shiksha Sanstha approach are examples of such experiments in language teaching in foundational stages. I worked with grade 4 Hindi and almost all the students were mostly comfortable with the script. But I soon realized that they struggled with writing independently. For instance, on the first day when I shared a worksheet based on the lesson we had just completed, the students found it difficult to answer. It was a picture composition and the students were asked to give it a title (sheershak, in Hindi). Not familiar with such questions, the students ended up interpreting it as “naming the image”, and ended up writing names they felt best suited the image (refer to image 1).

The next day, I went back to the class and discussed the question once again, but broke down the instructions for them. Talking is an important part of a language classroom. It helps students breakdown complex ideas into simpler ones, and arrive at original ideas on their own through appropriate scaffolding. This strategy worked really well. Students now understood what “title” meant, and how to “give a title” to an image.

I thus understood that in order for the remaining 14 language classes to be successful, I would need to adopt a more comprehensive approach to literacy. To ensure that they can write independently, merely increasing the time allocated to writing tasks was not enough, it had to be supported with appropriate instructions and activities.

Source: Menon, S., & Das, H. V. (2019). Comprehensive literacy instruction model in Indian classrooms (ELI Practitioner Brief No. 12). Early Literacy Initiative, Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Hyderabad.

The comprehensive literacy model thus came in handy. I tried to emphasize the different competencies in every class and provide ample opportunities to practice them.

Aligned activities

While the comprehensive literacy model helped me plan the lesson, some specific activities helped me drive the goal home.

Vocabulary

Sheershak or title, is a fairly simple word for an average class 4 student, but students in my class struggled with such simple words due to a lack of exposure. To build their vocabulary, I tried to use the word wall and some games in the class. To ensure that the students connect with the word wall, I got them to write on the wall on their own. I learnt this the hard way through my experiences of teaching during the Fellowship. I would have all my teaching material printed without involving the children, thinking that it would make my job easy, but not realizing that it would be counter-productive. Therefore, in this classroom, everyday students would add new words that we came across to the wall, and slowly, we had almost 30 words. What felt great was that the class teacher started using the wall for introducing vocabulary from other subjects as well, thus working on language across different content areas.

Apart from the word wall, strategies like using the same word in different contexts and allowing students to infer the meaning of the word helped drive critical thinking in the classroom (Image 4).

Writing tasks

Most students were scared of writing independently. They were used to copying answers from the board, and when asked to write independently in the worksheets, they would look for help. Talking and scaffolding bridged this gap. Guided writing also helped take away some apprehensions the students had. For instance, a lesson called “Rajim Mela” was written in the format of an informal letter to a friend. This seemed like a good opportunity to introduce letter writing. But introducing the format and content in an era where letters are not very common, was overwhelming. Therefore, we did guided letter writing to familiarize ourselves with the format first.

Guided writing helped students familiarize themselves with the format of a letter, which later helped them write letters independently.

Critical literacy

Comber et al. (2001) demonstrate how critical literacy can be used as a tool to build critical thinking and advance language learning in primary grades. Critical literacy refers to the ability to go beyond the surface level engagement with a text, and is heavily influenced by a child’s surroundings. The aims of language education are not just restricted to developing speaking and writing skills, but to use language to participate meaningfully in the society. One of the lessons I taught in Dhamtari was based on energy conservation. Some exercises in the book suggested writing down measures to conserve energy. I tried to make the exercise more contextually relevant by introducing a poster-making activity. Students had to make posters for different parts of the school related to the conservation of energy. This required them to think differently – how would they conserve energy in their classrooms, staff room, or at the hand wash tap. The posters made were put up in these designated areas, and the students talked about them in the assembly (Image 5).

I remember when I learnt letter writing in school. The prompts we were given were difficult to relate to. To make letter writing more interesting for the students, I framed specific questions for all of them, and included school-specific scenarios that they might be interested in. Luckily, I was teaching this lesson during Dussehra, and there was a fair nearby. Almost all the students had visited the fair and were excited to share their experiences. The letter writing task seemed like a good way to go about channelizing their excitement into writing. It also felt like a more purposeful activity.

Reflections

I had two goals in mind before I started teaching in Dhamtari, which stemmed from my learnings from my TFI fellowship and the semesters at APU. This included working on both dispositions and skills from my end. My failed engagements with the phonetic approach helped me learn the importance of a more comprehensive approach, which I put to work here. Secondly, the text used in class had to be mindfully selected as well. The Chhattisgarh SCERT textbooks had made attempts to root the literature in the Chhattisgarh context, which helped a lot. I could then contextualize it more with the help of the class teacher. The different activities I planned helped in maintaining the comprehensive approach to language education, as intended. This was a short engagement, and I hope to be able to put these to work in the future.

While the writing tasks gave me the most satisfaction, I realized that some students were either following the colloquial Hindi or Chhattisgarhi, their mother tongue. Despite similarities in Hindi and Chhattisgarhi, I was not sure if I could accept students’ writings in Chhattisgarhi. This was a dilemma I faced, and am still not sure what the right thing to do is. Perhaps there can be more structured time for Chhattisgarhi in school, with discussions around folk tales and local literary figures, where it would be acceptable to write and speak entirely in Chhattisgarhi, and Hindi can be limited to the Hindi classes.

Another challenge I faced was with assessments. While we had studied a little about formative assessments, I found it difficult to assess in every class. I later realized that the worksheets could have been designed better through constructive alignment (Biggs, J.), and developing a marking scheme for writing tasks could have helped students assess themselves, and become independent learners.

References

  • Biggs, J. (n.d.). Aligning teaching for constructing learning. https://www.johnbiggs.com.au
  • Chall, J. (1967/1983). Learning to read: The great debate. New York: McGraw-Hill.
  • Comber, B., Thompson, P., & Wells, M. (2001). Critical literacy finds a "place": Writing and social action in a low-income Australian grade 2/3 classroom. The Elementary School Journal, 101(4), 451-464.
  • Position Paper on Early Language and Literacy in India (2016). Select Chapters. Delhi: Ambedkar University and CARE India.
  • Wells, G. (2009). The Meaning Makers: Learning to Talk and Talking to Learn (2nd Ed), Bristol, UK. Multilingual Matters.
  • Yopp, H. K., & Yopp, R. H. (2000). Supporting phonemic awareness development in the classroom. The Reading Teacher, 54(2), 130-143.

Aayushi Singh is a recent graduate of MA Education from Azim Premji University. Previously, she taught grades 4 and 5 in a government school in Delhi as a Teach For India Fellow. Currently, she is working at Central Square Foundation as a project manager. She can be reached at aayushis140@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aayushi Singh
How can teachers balance listening, speaking, reading, and writing in primary classrooms? Aayushi Singh shares the challenges and insights from teaching Hindi in Chhattisgarh’s Dhamtari.

item.title: Is classroom attendance losing its meaning in today’s schools?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anuradha C

I open my front door at 6:20 am to pick up my milk packet and I see my neighbour rushing to drop his daughter off to the school bus. A groggy-eyed high school girl with big spectacles, still half-asleep, is gulping down her glass of milk as she awaits the lift. It takes her bus an hour and a half to reach school, so the ungodly hour of start has become routine. Out of curiosity, I asked if she felt all this hardship was worth it – whether she was really learning enough in school. She didn’t even pause before replying:

“Aunty, I learn more in one hour of self-study with the help of ChatGPT than in an entire week of class.”

Her response captures a sentiment that many children today quietly harbour: going to school feels less like a journey of discovery and more like a chore. Attendance numbers in many schools, especially high schools, are dropping, and not always because children are lazy or distracted. There are structural reasons – long commutes, unproductive classroom hours, the additional burden of after-school tuition, and a mismatch between how children learn best and how classrooms are run.

The attendance dilemma

School attendance has traditionally been considered the bedrock of education. But in recent years, administrators and parents alike have noticed worrying patterns:

  • According to a UNESCO report (2022), 15-20% of secondary school students in urban India skip classes at least once a week.
  • In the US, the National Center for Education Statistics (2023) reported that over 30% of students were chronically absent (missing more than 15 days in a school year).
  • Teachers themselves admit that even when students are physically present, many are “mentally absent,” with attention spans stretched thin (Willingham, 2021).

This isn’t just about numbers. It’s about how children perceive the value of time spent in school. If they feel the return on investment is poor, attendance naturally becomes less meaningful.

Why students find school a bore

1. The commute factor

For many children, the school day doesn’t start with the bell – it starts with the bus ride. A one-to-two-hour commute each way isn’t unusual in large cities (Singh, 2020). Imagine spending three hours daily just to reach a classroom, where the payoff feels underwhelming. By the time they arrive, fatigue has already dulled their ability to learn.

2. Unproductive classroom hours

Modern children are digital natives. They are used to interactive platforms, bite-sized learning, and instant answers. Compare this with the typical classroom, where:

  • The teacher explains at one pace, often not matching each child’s needs.
  • A large chunk of time is spent on roll call, discipline, or transitions between periods.
  • Rote learning dominates, leaving little space for curiosity or creativity (NCERT, 2021).

In short, the school day often feels bloated but thin on true learning.

3. Tuition culture

Ironically, many parents (and students) have come to believe that “real” learning happens after school – in tuition centers or coaching classes. In India, surveys show that over 70% of high school students attend some form of private tuition (ASER, 2022). School becomes a place for attendance and marks, while tuition becomes the place for understanding.

4. The “ROI” mindset

Children today are sharper at evaluating the worth of their time. When a high schooler says she learns more in one hour of self-study than in five days of lectures, she’s not exaggerating. Personalized learning tools, YouTube explainers, and AI tutors like ChatGPT offer immediate clarity, while classrooms often feel slow and repetitive.

What educationists say

Many educationists are openly questioning the traditional classroom model.

  • Sugata Mitra, known for the “Hole in the Wall” experiment, has long argued that children learn best when they are curious and self-directed, not when they are compelled to sit silently in rows (Mitra, 2012).
  • Salman Khan, founder of Khan Academy, stresses the importance of mastery-based learning, where students progress at their own pace instead of being forced to move with the class average (Khan, 2012).
  • Indian education reformer Sonam Wangchuk has repeatedly emphasized that schools should connect learning with life, not just textbooks and exams (Wangchuk, 2019).

These voices reflect the growing recognition that classrooms are not broken, but outdated.

Towards better attendance (and better learning)

Improving attendance isn’t about forcing children into classrooms. It’s about making classrooms worth attending. Here are some remedial measures schools can adopt:

1. Flexible school timings

Not every child learns best at 7 am. Starting schools later – say at 8:30 or 9:00 am – has been shown to improve both attendance and academic performance in multiple studies (American Academy of Pediatrics, 2014).

2. Smaller commutes, smarter locations

Urban planners and school boards can encourage neighborhood schooling or hybrid models where students attend fewer days in person but with more meaningful activities.

3. Active, engaging classrooms

Instead of long lectures, classrooms should adopt methods like:

  • Flipped classrooms, where students learn the basics at home via videos and use class time for problem-solving.
  • Project-based learning, where subjects connect to real-world applications.
  • Peer learning, encouraging collaboration instead of competition.

4. Integrating technology thoughtfully

Rather than competing with AI tutors, schools can integrate them. Imagine a math teacher assigning ChatGPT explanations as prep work, then using class time to tackle advanced problems. This way, the classroom amplifies rather than repeats learning.

5. Reducing tuition dependency

If schools genuinely deliver high-quality, personalized teaching, the dependence on tuition will reduce. This requires better teacher training, smaller class sizes, and more accountability in the system.

Ending on a positive note

The future isn’t all gloomy. Across the world, schools experimenting with new models are seeing encouraging results.

One story stands out: a 12-year-old boy in Bengaluru used to dread his daily 90-minute commute and the monotony of school. His parents shifted him to a neighbourhood school with project-based learning. Suddenly, his attendance improved, not because anyone forced him, but because he was excited to go. He began working on a solar-powered toy car as part of a science project, proudly presenting it to his classmates.

When asked if school still felt like a chore, his answer was simple: “No, I look forward to it. It feels like I’m learning something real now.”

And isn’t that the point? Attendance, after all, isn’t just about bodies in classrooms – it’s about minds engaged, curious, and alive with learning. When schools recognize this, children will no longer count the minutes wasted, but the hours well spent.

References

  • American Academy of Pediatrics. (2014). School start times for adolescents. Pediatrics, 134(3), 642-649.
  • Annual Status of Education Report (ASER). (2022). ASER 2022: Beyond Basics. ASER Centre.
  • Khan, S. (2012). The One World Schoolhouse: Education Reimagined. Twelve.
  • Mitra, S. (2012). Beyond the Hole in the Wall: Discover the Power of Self-Organized Learning. TED Books.
  • National Center for Education Statistics. (2023). Chronic absenteeism in U.S. public schools. U.S. Department of Education.
  • NCERT. (2021). National Education Policy Implementation Guidelines. NCERT Publications.
  • Singh, R. (2020). Urban education and commute challenges in Indian metros. Indian Journal of Education Studies, 15(2), 45-58.
  • UNESCO. (2022). Global Education Monitoring Report 2022. UNESCO Publishing.
  • Wangchuk, S. (2019). Education for life, not just livelihood. Lecture at IIT Delhi.
  • Willingham, D. (2021). Why don’t students like school? (2nd ed.). Jossey-Bass.

Anuradha C is an IT industry drop-out after several years of slogging and money-making. She is now working freelance as a corporate technical trainer and content writer. She is hoping to channelize her passion for writing into a satisfying experience for herself and a joyous experience for her readers. She can be reached at anuradhac@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anuradha C
When a high schooler says she learns more in an hour with ChatGPT than a week in class, it’s time to rethink attendance, writes Anuradha C.

item.title: Enriching mathematics education through indigenous knowledge systems
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Hemen Dutta

Integrating indigenous knowledge systems into mathematics education aligns with NEP 2020’s vision by promoting inclusive, culturally relevant learning, where students can connect traditional knowledge with modern concepts, fostering critical thinking, problem-solving, and contextual understanding of mathematical ideas rooted in local cultures. Indigenous knowledge systems are deeply rooted in the cultural practices, observations, and interactions of communities with their environment. These systems are not static; they evolve over generations as a reflection of the complex relationship between humans and Nature. Mathematics is an integral part of these systems, closely tied to activities such as agriculture, weaving, transportation, and architecture. Understanding these connections can significantly change the way mathematics is taught and learned, moving beyond memorizing formulas and rules to a deeper approach that helps students see how mathematics works in real life.

Indigenous practices can offer a powerful framework for integrating mathematical concepts with real-life applications from early education onward, using culturally relevant pedagogical approaches to enhance engagement, deepen understanding, and create meaningful connections to everyday experiences. For instance, local farming practices often involve geometric reasoning, such as calculating rice field areas and optimizing water distribution. Traditional methods, though intuitive, rely on Euclidean geometry to maximize land use and ensure efficient irrigation. By analyzing these practices, students can connect mathematics to real-world applications, enhancing their understanding and fostering innovative problem-solving skills. Textile weaving in many communities showcases mathematical concepts such as symmetry and fractions, offering a meaningful way for students to be introduced to these ideas. Likewise, ancient indigenous navigation systems, based on the observations of stars, ocean currents, and wind patterns, can help students learn trigonometric principles. Exploring how early navigators used angular measurements to determine their course can provide an engaging approach to teaching trigonometry, demonstrating its historical significance and real-world applications.

Integrating indigenous knowledge into formal education can greatly enhance critical mathematical thinking as well. Indigenous storytelling traditions often contain numerical riddles, logical puzzles, and real-world problem-solving scenarios, providing students with an engaging and enjoyable way of developing reasoning skills. For instance, in many indigenous cultures, stories that center around resource management, such as the fair distribution of food, are common. These narratives present situations where students must apply mathematical principles like division, fractions, and ratios to understand and solve problems. The stories not only make these concepts more relatable but also create lasting memories, as the problems and solutions are often tied to the values and practices of the community. This approach to learning nurtures a deeper understanding of mathematics, encouraging students to think critically and make connections between various ideas, rather than simply memorize formulas or procedures.

Indigenous knowledge can offer valuable opportunities for students to expand their mathematical understanding by linking it to sustainable practices. Many traditional methods, passed down through generations, can be linked to mathematics in ways that promote environmental sustainability. For instance, indigenous communities practicing sustainable fishing use observations of fish populations, breeding cycles, and migration patterns, which can be used to introduce statistical reasoning. Similarly, indigenous water conservation techniques, such as rainwater harvesting, rely on local geography, rainfall patterns, and seasonal changes to design efficient storage and distribution systems, incorporating calculations of volume, surface area, and geometry. These real-world applications can help students see how mathematical concepts contribute to addressing global challenges like ecological conservation and environment sustainability.

Indigenous knowledge can be integrated into mathematics education through collaborative approaches that engage educators, community experts, cultural practitioners, and students. Field-based activities, storytelling, and hands-on tasks can be used to bring indigenous knowledge to life in the classroom, making mathematical concepts more tangible and meaningful. For instance, collaborating with local artists can enable students to explore the mathematical principles embedded in traditional crafts like pottery or weaving. Learning alongside artisans not only deepens students’ mathematical understanding but also connects them to the cultural heritage behind these practices. This approach can also foster a sense of pride in their local traditions while broadening their perspective on how mathematics can be used in everyday life.

The role of technology in connecting indigenous knowledge with mathematics is also crucial. Digital platforms can serve as powerful tools for documenting and analyzing traditional practices, creating opportunities for students to engage with these methods in innovative ways. For instance, an app could simulate traditional agricultural techniques, helping students explore geometric patterns in planting and crop distribution. Virtual platforms could host weaving studios where students learn about symmetry and pattern creation in indigenous textile practices. These technological tools can be used to make learning more accessible and interactive while bridging traditional knowledge with formal education and connecting cultural heritage to mathematical concepts.

Integrating indigenous knowledge systems into mathematics education, however, may present challenges, including the risk of oversimplifying or misrepresenting local traditions. Moreover, there may be resistance to incorporating indigenous knowledge into mainstream curricula, as it may be perceived as incompatible with standardized educational frameworks. To address these concerns, community-driven approaches can be useful in ensuring that indigenous perspectives are respected and accurately represented. Educators may need training in culturally responsive teaching strategies and can benefit from collaborating with local communities to develop lessons that authentically reflect indigenous knowledge systems. Policymakers can enable this integration by providing essential support and resources, ensuring that indigenous knowledge is meaningfully incorporated into mainstream education.

In conclusion, integrating indigenous knowledge systems into mathematics education is not just about enriching the curriculum but about transforming how mathematics is taught and learned. By grounding mathematical concepts in local cultural and environmental contexts, students may develop a deeper, more practical understanding of the subject. This approach can foster critical thinking, cultural appreciation, and a sense of responsibility toward sustainability. Moreover, it may help bridge the gap between tradition and modernity, ensuring that indigenous wisdom continues to inform and inspire future generations. Embracing indigenous knowledge systems in mathematics education not only preserves their rich legacy but also maximizes the potential of mathematics as a powerful tool for understanding and shaping the world.

Dr. Hemen Dutta is with the Department of Mathematics, Gauhati University, Guwahati.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Hemen Dutta
What if geometry, fractions, and trigonometry could be learned through weaving, farming, and navigation? According to Dr Hemen Dutta, indigenous knowledge systems make this possible.
