item.title: Addressing critical thinking in classrooms in the times of Artificial Intelligence
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Jaikishan Advani and Jeenath Rahaman


The influence of Artificial Intelligence (AI) is growing both in the education sector and in our daily lives. It is becoming challenging for teachers to prevent the use of AI in the classroom with more and more students employing it for writing assignments, essays, problem solving, etc. Teachers often feel conflicted about the efforts they have to put into designing and grading assignments and activities when students employ AI tools to supply readymade answers. The way AI is being employed by students is leading to an erosion of learning.

AI can be useful as it provides quick personalized responses, which would otherwise have taken a lot of time and effort. But, the drawback of this is that it leads to cognitive offloading. Some studies point out that this can result in long-term effects on memory, thinking abilities, logical reasoning, and problem solving capabilities. In addition, there are a lot of issues such as biases in AI responses, incorrect responses, and possibilities of indoctrination, and hallucinations caused by AI usage, which can affect the very objectives of learning.

On the other hand, there are scholars who also argue that AI has a lot of potential to enhance critical thinking skills. For instance, it can effectively create innovative problems or give different perspectives on a topic being taught. Students can use AI to clear their doubts by asking thoughtful and creative questions. However, using AI for getting critical responses depends on what we ask it to provide, therefore, there is high likelihood that it can give us an illusion of critical thinking without it actually being so. Another major problem concerning AI usage is that it commodifies learning into ready-to-consume packaged responses, which is antithetical to the idea of learning. Learning involves engagement, through a process of constructing ideas, interconnecting facts, critical analysis, and building a story from one's experience. All these aspects are lacking in how AI is being currently employed.

Amongst so many problems concerning AI usage, the main challenges include questions such as, should – or can – AI be totally banned in the classroom or is there a way we can use it to enhance learning? What is the pedagogical role of AI? How can we prevent AI from replacing authentic learning?

Here's how.

Teachers can employ AI to help students find alternative perspectives on their own. Students can employ AI to interact and find out more about their topics of interest. Teachers can help students use AI as an object-to-think (question, analyze, agree, or disagree with), rather than as an object-to-copy responses from. They can also initiate discussions in their classrooms about the perspectives students build using AI tools and address the learning gaps. The issues concerning biases, AI led hallucinations, and indoctrination can be addressed by an active teacher-student discussion. This will give teachers and students an opportunity to correct the incorrect or partially correct notions they may have developed because of AI.

There are many other ways as well in which AI can be employed appropriately in classroom spaces to enhance critical thinking. For example, students can compare their own responses with responses generated from AI tools, then point to biases or ways to improve their own understanding. AI tools can provide information, activities, and problems for students to analyze, apply higher-order thinking, and enhance critical thinking skills. Students can try and find connections and enhance conceptual understanding using AI tools. They can also use AI to analyze book chapters and articles to get summaries and find different aspects they may have overlooked. Teachers can employ AI tools to determine student learning gaps and as a pedagogic tool for personalized learning. AI tools can also be employed in the classroom for group discussions and debates.

AI is good at reproducing mechanical qualities of the mind, such as generating texts, information, concepts, and stories. But the human essence cannot be reproduced. Everyone has their own experiences and upbringing which shape their minds. These cannot be replaced easily by a machine or a technology. Hence, teachers can bring in their own as well as students’ diverse experiences and social contexts to enhance critical thinking while employing AI.

The role of critical thinking becomes very important while employing AI. The teacher’s role is central in dealing with the challenges concerning AI usage in education. Eventually, we may witness a time when AI will have advanced to such an extent that it will overpower humans in all aspects including critical thinking. It is frightening to imagine such a future. AI should be designed to serve human interests and assist teachers. When profits conflict with human interests, there will be a problem. We need to preserve human essence and sharpen our critical thinking while employing AI and take steps to control AI when it causes harm to human interests.

Jaikishan Advani is a Research Associate currently working with Dr. Jeenath Rahaman, Assistant Professor at Azim Premji University, Bhopal, in a research project on the role of Artificial Intelligence in education. The authors can be reached at jaikishan.advani@apu.edu.in and jeenath.rahaman@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Jaikishan Advani and Jeenath Rahaman
Students turn to AI for essays, problem-solving, and summaries – but is learning being lost in the process? Jaikishan Advani and Jeenath Rahaman write about how classrooms can use AI without losing critical thinking skills.

item.title: From tamarind to sacred groves
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat

As we approached the temple, we saw fallen pillars and slabs.

“This is a beautiful temple. See the carvings on the wall. This must have been constructed during the Chola period. We have monuments like this near our hometown,” Madhavi said.

“It is ruined. Do not enter. Bushes have grown inside. It has not been maintained,” Sujatha cautioned the children.

“This must have been Lord Krishna’s temple. See the carvings. Krishna is playing the flute, and he is dancing on the snake,” Anand pointed to the figures on the wall.

“But look inside here. It seems to have been some goddess’ temple. The idol is damaged. We have many monuments like this that require attention. Some people say it was built during the Vijaynagara Empire. That is today’s Hampi,” I said.

“I know about the Vijayanagara Empire. Krishnadevaraya was a famous emperor. Gold and precious stones were traded in the open market during that time. Is it not?”Anand asked.

“True, probably one of his chieftains (palegara), who ruled this area, built this temple,” I said.

Rini called everyone, “Look here, beautiful flowers. See the black ant doing a circus underneath the flower.”

“This plant is called Senna hirsuta. Due to its hairy nature, it is called hirsuta. It is native to South American countries, but has naturalized here. I think the ant is getting nectar from the flowers, so it is climbing up the stem,” I replied.

Vivan said, “See the ant hill down there. How nicely it is built. We don’t notice these things in our everyday life.”

“Look at those bushes. What is that head upon head?” Shalini moved towards the bushes.

“Nice observation. The head-like structure is a cluster of flowers. It is called the Lion's ear, perhaps because of its bright orange colour. It belongs to the tulsi plant family. Take a look at the shape of the flower. Crush the leaf and smell it,’ I said.

Anand plucked a leaf and said, “Yes, some mint kind of aroma. But you can see pink and white Lantana on the other side. Even the Lantana leaf is fragrant, but it is a different fragrance.”

“Yes dear, the essential oil content is different in these plants, that is how it smells different,” I replied.

“What are essential oils?” Rini asked.

“They are concentrated plant extracts that retain the natural smell and flavour of their source, which are water repellent and easily vaporize, like eucalyptus oil. They are often used in aroma therapy,” I replied.

“My granny used to rub eucalyptus oil under my ears and on the forehead when I was suffering from a cold. I like the fragrance,” Shilpa said.

“Lantana grows extensively everywhere. These are found planted along borders as it is not eaten by grazing animals. It produces many different coloured flowers. I like it, but it is a threat to natural vegetation and is invading the forest area too. I read about this somewhere. Is it true?” Madhavi asked.

“Yes, it is an invasive species. It grabs nutrients from the soil and grows very fast. Other local plants are not able to compete with it. So, the insects, animals, and birds that are dependent on local plants for food and shelter are threatened.”

“What about tamarind? Is it local or foreign?” Vivan asked smiling.

“The scientific name for tamarind is Tamarindus indica. This suggests it originated in India, but it is also said that it is native to tropical Africa and may have been later naturalized in the Asian subcontinent,” I replied.

As we walked further, we came to a huge forked tree. In some places, the trunk was hollow, but it had green leaves on the branches.

“This is surprising, is it a single tree or many trees? Difficult to understand,” Shalini said.

“Maybe a single tree branched at the base, and other trees grew around it,” Madhavi said.

“See, a group of people is standing near that big tree, they must be college students,” Sujatha said.

“Botany, zoology, and ecology students often visit here. Let us go and see what they are doing,” I said.

I recognized Prof. Prabhakar from the Government Science College as we approached them. He acknowledged me and introduced me to his students.

I, too, introduced my Backyard Group members to the college group. Prof. Prabhakar made everyone sit in a circle and explained about the grove.

“This conservation site is a heritage site. It has been protected by the locals over the years. There are many conserved sites like this in India and other parts of the world, too. ‘Glory of Allapalli’ is in the Gadchiroli district of Maharashtra. It is famous for its ethnicity, valuable trees, and medicinal plants. ‘Dhotrey’ in the Darjeeling district of West Bengal is known for high-value medicinal plants. ‘Majuli’ is an island in the Brahmaputra River in Assam. The fertile wetlands are home to many resident and migratory birds. Like this, 47 biodiversity heritage sites have been identified in India.”

“Are there any similar kinds of conservation sites notified by the government?”one student asked.

Prabhakar said, “Some forests have been conserved in the name of a god or goddess. They are called sacred groves. You can find them in many parts of the world. They are home to various plants and animals. Their belief in the local god led people to think that everything in the forest belonged to that god, which is why hunting and harvesting are prohibited in such sites. There is limited access in some sacred groves to collect fallen twigs or fruits. In others, the collection of any material is prohibited. Nandini ma’am, who worked in sacred groves, can tell you more.”

I continued, “Sacred groves are undisturbed forests and community reserves under the Wildlife Protection Act. In some places,a restricted path allows people to visit the forest path to perform rituals and return the same day without disturbing the area. These groves serve as a reserve for many rare flora and fauna, in the midst of rural or even urban landscapes. Around 14000 sacred groves have been reported across India. But experts say there are many more sacred groves. Threats to the groves include urbanization and over-exploitation of resources.”

“Why do we need to conserve these sacred groves? Are they relevant in today’s context?” a student asked.

“An undisturbed forest is the basis for ecological services, such as providing fresh water, good humus soil, and pollinators for crops. To maintain ecological balance, it is necessary to have some forest near every human settlement. Probably, our ancestors thought about this and reserved an area near human habitation in the name of a god, so that people would not use that land for recreation, collection, or expansion of townships,” I explained.

Anand asked, “Can you tell us some of your observations about sacred groves?”

“In Karnataka, sacred groves are called “devara kadu,” which means god’s forest. In Kerala as “kavu”, in Tamil Nadu as “kovil kadu,” in Maharashtra as “deorai”,in Sikkim “gumpa forests”. In this way, everywhere a forest is associated with god. In case anyone bypasses the customary laws and harvests something from the sacred grove, it is a punishable offence. He is made to pay a penalty and beg for pardon in the Panchayat. I have seen trees that are as tall as a seven to eight-story building and trunks so wide that even 10 people are not enough to hug the tree. So we can imagine how old such trees are,” I said.

“To understand it better, let us play a game. All of you write the names of the trees you know,” Prof. Prabhakar instructed.

Everyone started writing.

Suggested activities

  1. Make a list of essential oils used in cosmetics.
  2. Name aromatic plants growing near your house.

References

https://indiaflora-ces.iisc.ac.in/herbsheet.php?id=7016&cat=13

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamarind

Nandini Dholepat is a botanist who has been involved in teaching and research for more than 25 years. Currently, she is engaged as a consultant for Intellectual Property Rights for Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology, Bengaluru, and is a visiting faculty member at RIWATCH, Roing, Arunachal Pradesh. She can be reached at nandinidholepat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat
The Backyard Discovery group observed tamarind trees and sacred groves, learning how history, ecology, and community conservation connect in our everyday landscapes.

item.title: The power of fiction: how stories can shape emotional intelligence and curiosity
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sunita Lad Bhamray

Storybooks have been considered a treasured part of childhood for decades. They have been the very first form of imparting knowledge, wonder, and hope to children by introducing them to magical worlds, courageous heroes, and timeless adventures. Beyond mere entertainment, these stories play a critical role in shaping children’s emotional intelligence, creativity, and cognitive development.

However, with the increasing dominance of digital screens, social media, and short-form content, children’s engagement with books is rapidly diminishing. With easy access to smartphones, social media, and gaming platforms, children today consume content in bite-sized, fast-paced formats, leaving little room for long-form reading like books.

The impact of this shift is already evident. A 2024 survey highlighted that only 34.6% of children aged 8 to 18 in India enjoy reading during their free time. This alarming statistic has pushed educators and policymakers to take action. And while policy-level efforts are essential, parents and educators have an equally critical role to play in reigniting a love for reading among children, particularly fiction books.

The importance of reintroducing fiction books to children cannot be overstated. Here are five ways how fiction books can impact a child’s development:

1. Boosts creativity and imagination

Storybooks open the door to magical worlds, giving children the freedom to let their imaginations work. This process of imaginative thinking helps them develop creativity, encouraging them to think differently. This also inspires them to create their own stories, ideas, or alternate endings to the plots they read. This natural storytelling ability translates into better writing, communication, and problem-solving skills as they grow older.

By constantly engaging with imaginative content, children learn to think outside the box, dream big, and approach real-life situations with curiosity and innovation. A strong imagination, once nurtured, stays with children for life, helping them become innovative thinkers and lifelong learners.

2. Builds critical thinking and problem-solving skills

Fiction exposes children to imaginary yet relatable challenges, allowing them to draw parallels with real-life situations. When they follow complex plots and anticipate what happens next, it automatically sharpens their problem-solving skills. Whether it's resolving conflicts with friends, tackling schoolwork, or making personal decisions, children learn to approach problems with clarity and resilience. By constantly engaging with problem-solving narratives, fiction helps shape children’s ability to think logically, stay focused, and approach challenges with a solution-oriented mindset. Over time, this cognitive development strengthens their decision-making skills, creative thinking, and capacity to navigate complex situations in real life.

3. Fosters empathy, emotional intelligence, and cultural awareness

One of the most profound impacts of fiction is its ability to humanize experiences. When children immerse themselves in stories about diverse characters from different backgrounds, experiencing joy, grief, conflict, or triumph, they subconsciously develop empathy. Experiencing the world through a character’s lens helps children understand human emotions and social complexities, building their emotional intelligence.

Fiction also fosters social and cultural awareness. Stories that portray different socio-economic backgrounds, cultural settings, or social challenges, subtly introduce children to the concept of privilege, inclusivity, and diversity. This early exposure helps raise compassionate, socially aware individuals who are more likely to build meaningful relationships, embrace inclusivity, and navigate diverse environments with kindness and understanding.

4. Improves focus and patience

Fiction books demand sustained attention. When children read a story, they must follow the plot, connect with characters, and process the story arc. Reading helps rebuild their cognitive stamina, allowing them to stay engaged for longer periods without distraction.

Over time, children who read regularly develop a higher capacity for concentration – a critical skill that supports academic performance, classroom learning, and overall mental resilience. Reading also helps reduce restlessness and improves their ability to complete tasks with greater attention to detail.

5. Teaches resilience and adaptability

Fiction often mirrors real-life challenges, depicting characters who face obstacles, failures, or difficult situations. By following the story arc, children begin to understand that challenges are a natural part of life.

Additionally, fiction teaches adaptability. As plotlines shift and unexpected events unfold, children see how characters adjust, make decisions, and find solutions. This experience subtly equips them to navigate changes in their own lives, whether it’s adjusting to a new school, making new friends, or coping with failures.

By normalizing failure and problem-solving through storytelling, fiction nurtures strong, adaptable, and emotionally resilient individuals.

A collective effort to revive reading habits

In a world dominated by instant gratification, social media, and fast-paced content, children’s ability to concentrate is rapidly declining. A 2022 survey revealed that 49% of parents in urban India reported their children aged 9 to 13 spent over three hours daily on activities like gaming, watching videos, or using social media. This constant exposure to fragmented information has impacted their ability to focus, read, or engage deeply with content.

The Government of India’s initiatives, such as setting up libraries in schools and promoting National Reading Day, are also a step in the right direction. However, the solution lies not only in policy changes but also in collective efforts from parents, teachers, and authors.

Parents can foster a love for reading by introducing storytelling sessions at home, curating a diverse collection of books, or setting aside dedicated reading time. Teachers, on the other hand, can incorporate book-based discussions, book reading events, reading clubs, and literary events to make books a part of everyday school culture.

The path forward: growing readers into thinkers

The value of stories in a child’s life extends far beyond entertainment and literacy. Stories ignite curiosity, build imagination, cultivate empathy, and enhance cognitive capabilities, all of which contribute to shaping well-rounded, emotionally intelligent individuals. In a world where digital content is overwhelming young minds, books offer children a chance to slow down, reflect, and build their inner world of imagination and creativity.

Sunita Lad Bhamray is a Singapore-based author, playwright, and screenwriter celebrated for her transformative storytelling and dedication to inspiring change. With a rich career spanning literature, theatre, and film, Sunita’s work consistently addresses important social issues while captivating audiences of all ages. Her ability to craft narratives that provoke thought, foster empathy, and encourage meaningful dialogue has earned her recognition as a versatile and impactful storyteller.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sunita Lad Bhamray
In an age of screens and instant content, fiction books nurture the reader’s imagination and help them focus. Sunita Lad Bhamray writes about why stories are essential for raising emotionally intelligent and creative thinkers.

item.title: On experiencing learning experiences!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

I recently ate at a special Gujarati thali restaurant that has been around since before independence. It is special, not only because of the absolutely indulgent food they serve but the manner in which they serve you. It feels like home, with a team of people serving you the hottest puri that comes out of the kitchen, explaining to you the seasonal ingredients that are part of each dish, and they don’t let you leave till you have tasted every single dessert they have to offer! What an experience that was and it made me think about what it is in an experience that makes us remember it. Why? Because as an educator, I see ‘teaching’ as an act of creating ‘learning experiences’ for students. Experiences they will enjoy, cherish and REMEMBER, not to vomit in a test, but connect to other experiences that life throws at them.

There are a couple of threads here that I’m going to chase. One is that of experience itself and what it entails. Second, how the arts enable a perception within us that influences our context through which we experience life. And finally, in the context of education, to view learning as an act of engaging with life through dynamic experiences and how this view can bring about a pedagogical shift.

An experience

John Dewey has written at length about the nature of experience in his book Art As Experience (1934), where he says that an ordinary experience, like living life, occurs continuously through the interaction between a live creature (an individual) and its environment. However, ‘an experience’ occurs and stands out in an ongoing experience because it is marked from what happened before and what came after; it has a beginning, middle, and an end. For example, ‘that meal’, ‘that conversation’, ‘that storm’ and so on.

Dewey adds another interesting layer here that the background or context of the world the individual inhabits shapes their experience, which is impregnated with one’s uniqueness. For instance, an individual’s interests, dispositions, physical, cognitive, and emotional capacities and so on (Dewey, 1934) will influence how they experience something. Thus, different people will explore an experience through their uniqueness, giving rise to different perceptions.

From a Deweyan point of view, this unique experience that he calls an ‘aesthetic experience’ roughly follows a structure. The experience is set off by some material or a stimulus like opening a book, viewing an image, beginning a meal, or starting a conversation. The effect of this stimulus changes depending on the context of the individual engaging with it, and it merges into new ones as the experience proceeds. When these different parts form a distinctive unity that stands out from the general experiential stream of our lives, Dewey says the experience in question is aesthetic.

For a moment, let’s see how this can pan out in the context of learning. For a child, the general experiential stream of their life is going to school every day. But the moment something different occurs – like a quarrel during lunch, a teacher wearing something special, a math class conducted on the playground, a language lesson that begins with a puzzle or a game – a certain stimulus is set off with the possibility of creating a unique and aesthetic experience.

Aesthetic and non-aesthetic experiences

Dewey uses the example of a journey to differentiate between an aesthetic experience and a non-aesthetic one. In an aesthetic experience, means and ends coalesce. Means are an end in the aesthetic. While in non-aesthetic experiences, means are mechanical steps used to get to the desired end. There is delight in moving about and seeing in an aesthetic journey, while in a non-aesthetic one the only objective is to reach the destination and any way to shorten the trip is welcome.

Young students learning mathematical operations for the first time will gladly spend hours repeatedly adding the number 3, 20 times (3 + 3 = 6 + 3 = 9 + 3 = 12…), making mistakes, starting over, helping each other, feeling the joy of getting it right, and then slowly feeling the tedium of the exercise when they must do the same for number 4 and 5 and so on. As it is a new experience for them, they will jump right into it, but there is a moment in this journey where it can become mechanical with energies dispersing and distractions coming in. Before such a moment occurs, the teacher must introduce the concept of multiplication, triggering off a new experiential cycle of discovery.

Well-designed learning experiences where the teacher is acutely conscious of how and when she plays with the material or stimulus that sets off experiences for students to be engaged and invested in, while being mindful of the context in which they will be experiencing it, is a palpable shift in pedagogy.

The arts enabling aesthetic experiences

We are hoping to create educational models that follow a curriculum framework where the focus is on moving about, seeing, exploring, being curious, and engaging with the world for learning to happen; where arts-based approaches full of material and external stimulus play an important role in the learning process. However, educator and philosopher Gert Biesta is critical of the growing attention the arts has in educational circles these days. He highlights it as a serious concern that schools want art on their curriculum because it is useful: “Playing music will make you better at algebra, dance will make you better at geometry, theatre will improve your self-regulation skills,” and so on (Biesta, & Skregelid, 2022, Art keeps us in and with the World).

Biesta points out that when we talk about the usefulness of the arts for generating particular educational “outcomes,” there is a disappearance of the arts from arts education as the focus is on what arts education ‘makes or produces’ instead of it focussing on ‘what it makes possible’. This directly co-relates to the non-aesthetic experience we discussed earlier, which focuses on the end destination, as described by Dewey.

So how can we talk about the importance of the arts without talking about its usefulness? Here, Biesta draws inspiration from artists who are, in different ways, always trying to figure out what it means to exist ‘in and with the world’ and what it means to be human and to live one’s life with its many dimensions, manifestations, and facets, knowing well that it is impossible to arrive at a definitive answer. What distinguishes art is that art always has a medium: sound, paint, stone, time, story, images, words, tone, rhythm, movement, and so on. So, in working with a “medium,” artists are stepping into a dialogue with the world outside of them.

‘I tend to think that the arts are relevant for education – which is different from saying that they are useful for education – because the arts themselves have the existential question, the question of what it means to exist in and with the world, as their central concern. And for me, this is also the central concern for education’ (Biesta, & Skregelid, 2022, Art keeps us in and with the World).

When a teacher brings in a stimulus or some material (a photograph, a news article, a book, an object) to start off a conversation in the class, this can be seen as the “medium” that gives the opportunity to students to engage with the world and the existential questions that come with it.

Experience. The arts. Where’s the teacher in all of this?

Biesta says that educational work is about ‘holding’ students in this difficult middle ground where they can meet the world and can meet themselves in relation to the world. Where they can encounter the difference between ‘fantasy’ and ‘reality’, ‘desire’ and ‘consequences of such desires’. This way of looking at education suggests that education is not about designing smooth, personalized learning trajectories that rush students swiftly towards the production of measurable learning outcomes. It rather suggests that if at its core, education takes on the existential question seriously, it seeks to interrupt as it “stages” an encounter between students and reality. This interruption must be followed by suspension, by slowing down, so there is time to meet this reality and work through the complexities and possible frustrations of such a meeting. At this stage, teachers need to provide sustenance through tools and support for holding students in that place. The arts offer this journey and for educators, the main task is to open up different “existential possibilities” for students – different ways in which they can exist, meet the world, and meet themselves in relation to the world.

However, in order for teachers to truly bring these nuanced ideas into their pedagogical approach, it is imperative for them to be inundated with learning experiences of this nature. Teacher development programs need to use the medium of the arts to hold teachers in that ‘difficult middle ground’ where they can meet the world and meet themselves in relation to the world. This will equip them to ask difficult questions, not fear interruptions but see them as opportunities to engage, to slow down and bring attention to such encounters, and finally learn what sustenance can look like preparing themselves to design similar learning experiences for students.

Tying it back to Dewey who said that individual contexts influence how we receive experiences around us, we can perhaps hope that engaging with the medium of the arts regularly can enable an aesthetic perception that leaves teachers open and ready to engage with the world, thus influencing their individual context, where the delight in seeing and exploring through the journey is more important than reaching a destination.

References

  • Dewey, J. (1934), Art As Experience.
  • Biesta, G. J. J. & Skregelid, L. (2022). Art keeps us in and with the World: Gert Biesta in Conversation with LisbetSkregelid.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
Education is more than measurable outcomes. Timira shares how classrooms can create experiences that students engage with and truly remember.

item.title: Leading by example: embracing servant and visible leadership in schools
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Tvara Srivastava and Disha Shrivastava

"Each day, you are leading by example. Whether you realise it or not, or whether it's positive or negative, you are influencing those around you." – Rob Liano

An organization is a reflection of its leadership. The school leader holds a significant position that shapes the school's functioning and students. If one goes by the standard definition of a leader, one can imagine a person leading from the front. In a school setup, this is the principal or headmistress, often someone with a stern look, sitting behind a big glass cabin, and whom the children are usually afraid to talk to.

But what happens when a leader is accessible or visible? Do the dynamics change? Do children find it more joyful to come to school? Do parents have a voice? Does the community thrive?

Student-centred leadership is an educational approach that puts students at the centre of the work that a school does, ensuring that students are prepared for learning and life. While leaders embody different leadership styles, the focus of this article is on two leadership styles that ensure the success of a student-centred leader: servant leadership and visible leadership. Understanding and applying these leadership styles ensures that leaders place students at the heart of their decision-making processes and that educational practices are tailored to students’ unique needs, aspirations, and challenges, empowering them to thrive academically, socially, and emotionally.

As humans, we are constantly looking for role models to shape our views, morals, and work. But how does the leader know who is falling behind? How do they cater to the needs of all if they are so far away and detached from everyone else? This is where the concept of servant leadership comes into play. In 1970, Robert K Greenleaf wrote the seminal essay, ‘The Servant as Leader,’ espousing that servant leaders supported the growth of their employees through inclusivity and a needs-based approach. This philosophy flipped the traditional definition of good leadership. (Insert above image here)

At its core, servant leadership is based on the principles of empathy, listening, and understanding people’s ways of functioning. The term was inspired by the book Journey to the East by Hermann Hesse (1954), in which a servant becomes the group’s leader because he could anticipate the group's needs and was accustomed to listening more than speaking.

A rigorous and evidence-based meta-study by Vivienne Robinson* evaluates the impact of different practices and activities of school leaders. This research suggests that the strongest way to impact student achievement is for leaders to promote and participate in teacher development. In schools, this translates to principals and other leaders anticipating, listening to, and catering to the needs of teachers. Effective schools often have schedules for training, and effective school leaders work closely with the teachers by providing them aids and means to enhance their capacities through periodic teacher training, coaching and professional development programmes. At Shishuvan School in Mumbai, for instance, Kavita Anand, the director at the time, had set-aside a budget for teacher professional development, which was reimbursed to teachers who would identify their own development needs.

Shubadra Shenoy, Principal of Little Angels International School, Mumbai, endorses this idea, sharing that her school has allocated a professional development budget for each teacher, addressing training needs identified by both the school and the teachers themselves. “The budget enabled teachers to pursue courses or training that enhance their teaching and learning skills, fostering professional growth. Empowering teachers in this way ultimately benefits students, as a well-equipped educator is better positioned to inspire and support student success.”

It becomes imperative for a leader to be visible irrespective of the leadership approach. A visible leader must enable growth in all stakeholders they work with, and also model that behaviour for each staff member to feel empowered in emulating it. “Leaders must go where learning is most important, and that is inside the classroom,” says Spokey Wheeler, co-founder and international director of Adhyayan Quality Education Services pvt. ltd. and a former head teacher in many schools in England, India, and abroad, When teachers and students see their leaders in their classrooms, it makes the classroom valuable and makes each person in the classroom feel valued.

A fine example of visible leadership was demonstrated by Paul Machado, former principal of Campion School, Mumbai. Paul would greet children with a warm smile and a quick conversation at the entrance gate every morning. Parents across the school spoke highly of this practice because they felt welcomed and confident about leaving their kids at school. A principal welcoming children is not a common practice. However, Paul thought that the practice gave him a deeper insight into what his students were feeling each day and into the pulse of the school, allowing him to cater to the needs of his students. Being a visible leader, Paul communicated to the school that his leadership is open and accessible.

“Great principals focus on improving the quality of the teachers within their buildings. By carefully hiring the best teachers, by supporting their efforts and their ambitions, by holding all staff members to high expectations, and by working to support the individual development of each professional, principals carefully impact student achievement.” – Todd Whitaker

In essence, effective school leadership is far more than just administrative; it is about being present, accessible, and genuinely committed to the growth of every individual within the school community. When leaders choose to lead by serving others and by making themselves visible – whether through daily greetings at the gate or active engagement in classrooms – they create an environment where students feel valued, teachers feel supported, and parents feel heard. This holistic approach fosters a thriving, inclusive, and vibrant school culture that places students firmly at its heart. As Rob Liano reminds us, leadership is influence, and every moment a school leader spends leading by example can profoundly shape the lives and futures of those they guide. By embracing servant and visible leadership, school leaders can ensure that their institutions remain places of growth, inspiration, and success for all stakeholders, leaving a lifelong, lasting impact on each child.

*Robinson, Viviane & Hohepa, Margie & Lloyd, Claire. (2009). School Leadership and Student Outcomes: Identifying What Works and Why Best Evidence Synthesis Iteration (BES)

Tvara Srivastav is a former Program Manager at Adhyayan. She is an Ashoka University graduate and Young India Fellow. She has worked in the education sector across domains and has a keen interest in education initiatives and reforms. She loves writing about day-to-day experiences on her personal blog.

Disha Shrivastava is CEO, Adhyayan Quality Education Services Pvt Ltd. She comes with over 16 years of work experience across corporates, nonprofits and startup domains. She has a strong fundraising, partnerships, strategic communications, and soft-skills expertise. A feminist advocate, she advocates and writes for gender rights. A storyteller at heart, she can be seen performing poetry and story recitals.

The authors can be reached at disha@adhyayan.asia.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Tvara Srivastava and Disha Shrivastava
What if the principal wasn’t just a figure behind a glass cabin? Tvara Srivastava and Disha Shrivastava write about how leaders who are accessible and present can transform school culture and learning.

item.title: Technological assessments
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

Education in India is constantly evolving, and the integration of technology has opened up exciting new avenues for teaching and learning. While traditional pen-and-paper assessments hold their value, computer-based assessments offer a dynamic and versatile alternative that can significantly enhance our approach to evaluating student understanding in middle school mathematics. The recent surge in digital tools, partly accelerated by the need for remote learning, has highlighted the potential of technology to personalize feedback, foster engagement, and even adapt assessments to individual student needs (Shute & Rahimi, 2017).

For many of us, the transition to incorporating technology in assessments might seem like a significant shift. However, the benefits it offers in terms of interactivity, personalized feedback, communication, and even sustainability are compelling. Let's explore some of the advantages and practical applications of computer-based assessments in math classrooms.

Enhanced interactivity and engagement

The digital realm provides a plethora of interactive platforms and online resources that can transform assessments from static exercises into engaging learning experiences.

  • Interactive question types: Unlike traditional multiple-choice questions on paper, computer-based assessments can incorporate drag-and-drop activities, matching exercises, simulations, and interactive graphs.
    • Example (Fractions): Instead of simply choosing the correct equivalent fraction, students could drag fraction tiles to visually represent equivalent fractions or use an interactive number line to locate and compare different fractions. Platforms like Khan Academy offer interactive exercises on fractions.
    • Example (Geometry): Students could manipulate virtual geometric shapes to explore their properties, match shapes with their names, or even construct simple geometric figures using online tools like GeoGebra.
  • Gamification elements: Incorporating game-like elements such as points, badges, and leaderboards can increase student motivation and make the assessment process more enjoyable. Platforms like Quizizz and Kahoot! allow teachers to create engaging quizzes and interactive lessons.
    • Example (Basic Operations): A timed online quiz with points awarded for correct answers and bonus points for speed can make practicing basic arithmetic operations more engaging.

Personalized and timely feedback

One of the most significant advantages of computer-based assessments is the potential for providing personalized and immediate feedback to students.

  • Automated scoring and feedback: Many online assessment tools can automatically score objective question types (multiple choice, true/false, matching) and provide instant feedback to students, highlighting correct and incorrect answers.
    • Example (Decimals): An online quiz on decimal place values can provide immediate feedback, indicating whether the student correctly identified the tenths, hundredths, or thousandths place in a given number.
  • Teacher-provided private feedback: Platforms like Google Classroom allow teachers to view student work, provide specific written or even audio feedback privately, fostering a more direct and supportive communication channel (Shute & Rahimi, 2017).
    • Example (Algebraic Equations): When students submit their solutions to algebraic equations online, teachers can provide targeted feedback on specific steps, highlighting errors in manipulation or suggesting alternative approaches.
  • AI-powered adaptive assessments: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in online assessment tools opens up exciting possibilities for adaptive testing. AI algorithms can adjust the difficulty of questions based on a student's performance in real-time, providing a more accurate measure of their understanding and catering to individual learning pace (Shute & Rahimi, 2017). Platforms like ALEKS utilize AI to personalize learning pathways and assessments.
    • Example (Ratios and Proportions): An AI-powered assessment might start with basic ratio problems. If a student answers correctly, the subsequent questions might involve more complex proportion problems or real-world applications. If they struggle, the system might offer simpler problems or review foundational concepts.

Enhanced communication and collaboration

Computer-based platforms can also facilitate better communication between teachers and students and even enable collaborative assessment activities.

  • Direct communication channels: Online platforms provide direct channels for teachers to communicate with individual students or groups, answer questions promptly, and provide clarifications.
    • Example: Students struggling with a concept during an online assessment can privately message the teacher for guidance, receiving immediate support without disrupting the entire class.
  • Group assessments and peer review: Online tools can facilitate group projects and assessments where students collaborate on shared documents or presentations. They can also be used for peer review activities, where students provide feedback on each other's work. Google Workspace offers tools like Google Docs and Slides for collaborative work.
    • Example (Data Interpretation): Students in different locations can work together on a shared Google Sheet to analyze a set of data and then create a collaborative presentation using Google Slides to present their findings.

Sustainability and efficiency

Beyond the pedagogical benefits, computer-based assessments can also contribute to a more sustainable and efficient learning environment.

  • Reduced paper consumption: By delivering and collecting assignments and assessments electronically, we can significantly reduce paper usage, contributing to environmental sustainability.
  • Streamlined grading and record-keeping: Many online assessment platforms offer features for automated grading (for certain question types) and efficient record-keeping, saving teachers valuable time and effort. Learning Management Systems (LMS) like Canvas and Moodle provide comprehensive tools for managing online assessments and grades.

Expanding assessment possibilities with AI

The integration of AI in education is rapidly evolving, and its application in assessments holds immense potential for personalization and deeper insights into student learning.

  • Automated feedback on open-ended questions: While still developing, AI-powered tools are emerging that can analyze student responses to open-ended questions, providing feedback on the clarity of their reasoning, the accuracy of their mathematical language, and the completeness of their solutions. Platforms like Gradescope utilize AI to assist with grading and providing feedback on handwritten work.
    • Example (Problem-Solving): An AI tool could analyze a student's written solution to a word problem, identifying the correct answer but also providing feedback on the logical flow of their steps or suggesting alternative, more efficient methods.
  • Identifying learning patterns and difficulties: AI algorithms can analyze student performance data across multiple assessments to identify common misconceptions, learning patterns, and areas where students consistently struggle. This information can be invaluable for teachers to tailor their instruction and provide targeted interventions.
  • Personalized learning paths and remediation: Based on AI-driven assessment insights, the system can automatically recommend personalized learning resources and activities to address individual student needs and provide targeted remediation. Platforms like Khan Academy already offer personalized learning paths based on student performance.

A balanced perspective

While the advantages of computer-based assessments are numerous, it's important to maintain a balanced perspective. Pen-and-paper assessments still hold value, particularly for assessing certain skills and providing a different mode of engagement. A blended approach, strategically integrating both computer-based and traditional assessments, is likely the most effective way forward.

Computer-based assessments are not the ultimate solution for all our assessment needs. However, they offer a powerful and evolving set of tools that can significantly enhance our ability to understand and support our middle school math students' learning journeys. By exploring and thoughtfully integrating these digital resources, we can create more engaging, personalized, and ultimately more effective assessment practices in our classrooms.

Reference

Shute, V., & Rahimi, S. (2017). Review of computer-based assessment for learning in elementary and secondary education. Journal of Computer Assisted Learning, 33(1), 1-19. http://myweb.fsu.edu/vshute/pdf/jcal.pdf

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Can math tests be fun, interactive, and tailored to each student? Monica Kochar writes about how digital tools and gamified assessments can transform evaluation in Indian classrooms.

item.title: Inspiring environmental consciousness
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ankita Rajasekharan

We find ourselves today, in a time where activism and action-taking is a political, environmental, social, and even an emotional stand. There are forests needing protection, lakes needing preservation, communal injustices needing to be challenged, climate movements needing amplification, the list is endless. Each generation may feel that the problems of their time are like ones never before, with the weight of the world resting heavily on their shoulders. It is in light of this that Mamata Pandya and Meena Raghunathan’s book, Inspirations, offers temporary respite and a boost of momentum, in learning of successes and stories of a different generation’s response to the environmental and social concerns of their time.

Mamata and Meena, having spent decades working at the Centre for Environment Education (CEE), were witness to several pioneering movements and experiments in education, agriculture, biodiversity documentation, rural wisdom, grassroots journalism, and more. Understandably, they are in deep awe of the many individuals they came by in their work and it shows in their writing! The book is underlined with cheerful narratives and youth-like admiration for who they rightfully call ‘trailblazers’. Their writing is marked with infectious glee, curiosity, and wonder for the India of the 70s and the citizen movements and actions of the time alongside experiments with nation-building in a post-independent country. The book is a remarkable collection of essays narrating the work, motivations, and institutional impact of 30 individuals who were in one way or another, associated with CEE. Some of these include AR Rao, Arvind Gupta, Kalpana Sharma, MK Prasad, MS Swaminathan, Salim Ali, SM Nair, and Vikram Sarabhai. Many of these names are well-known in the space of environmental conservation and social change. Some may not be household names, but their work continues to speak loudly to us even today. The book brings to us, all their stories in equal measure.

Educators and young people drawn to environmental conservation and awareness have much to benefit from familiarizing themselves with these individual journeys. Where the book truly shines is in the personal narratives of some of the conservationists. Be it the journey of Salim Ali from shooting sparrows as a child to observing them closely and documenting their behaviour as a scientific inquiry, or Arvind Gupta having fashioned his own toys from easily sourced materials as a child to creating a movement to offer the same with a layer of scientific wonder for the vast majority of rural children in the country, or of a walk the authors partook with HY Mohan Ram as he shared facts and anecdotes of various plants and trees, making a 5-7 minute walking path, a 2-hour long storytelling tour! These narratives can be deeply inspiring while humanizing the persons we otherwise know for their large-scale impacts and awards. In humbling everyday tales of interactions and experiences, one may be able to find the turning points in one’s own stories and go on to act on simple curiosities, not knowing the potential pathways they may lead one on. Especially for children in their late school years, educators may find these personalizing stories an inviting medium to talk further about the larger topics of sustainability, environmental campaigns, and policies.

Being set in the 70s and the decades after, the book also offers a unique perspective into what it meant to take a stand, challenge a system, and problem-solve under the umbrella of ‘nation-building’ in the context of a post-independence country. The journeys of these individuals are deeply intertwined with the historical context of the time they were born into. Many of these individuals came with the energy of having had parents who were freedom fighters and had a nation that could carve its path ahead in a multitude of ways. Many firsts were to be in the time to come – AR Rao set up India’s first Mathematical Laboratory, SM Nair was the first to be awarded a doctorate in Museology from MS University (Baroda), Luvkumar Khachar launched India’s first Nature Club movement for WWF India. The book also offers an interesting study of women pioneers of the time. Be it Kalpana Sharma with her vast reporting on social and political realities, including farmer suicides, religious fundamentalism, patriarchy and more, Sunita Narain’s activism that ‘combined science-based evidence with a sharp pro-poor perspective to mainstream environmental concerns in public policy and society’, Priyambada Mohanty-Hejmadi on the forefront of the movement to protect Olive Ridley turtles while also being a well-known proponent of the Odissi dance form!

Given the large-scale impact many of these visionaries have had on the environmental and sustainability movement in the country, many of the essays carry a long list of awards and recognitions they may have been bestowed with or institutions they may have established. Many of these tend to move into organizational jargon, award vocabulary, and abbreviations that may be overwhelming for a generic reader. Essays highlighting childhood influences, inspirations, impact of the socio-political context and experiments with anecdotes of both success and failures truly stand out. The lived realities of people, famous and not, go a long way in motivating the reader and helping them move from the thinking space into the action phase. The potential of individual passion mixed with community buy-in and upliftment seems unbounded in these narratives. We know more than ever, how much we need that kind of momentum and hope today! This collection of essays is a great place to get a feel of that.

Ankita Rajasekharan is an educator and nature enthusiast. She enjoys working with children on nature and art based explorations, spending time in the natural environment, observing and documenting small happenings in nature. She has been working as an educator for over 10 years now. She also writes from time to time at: https://earthlingideas.substack.com. She can be reached at 27.ankita@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ankita Rajasekharan
From Salim Ali’s bird observations to Arvind Gupta’s innovative toys, Ankita Rajasekharan reviews a book about those who influenced India’s environmental and social movements.

item.title: Federalism and the parliamentary system of democracy
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: We have been talking about federalism, bicameralism, separation of powers, the executive, legislature, and judiciary. All this seems to be resulting in a long and complex Constitution. I am still trying to figure out why the Constituent Assembly designed such a complex system of governance.

We kept speaking of concentration of power as the reason for all this. What is this power? Why do we need to be so worried about it, especially since we also keep saying democracy is basically about power residing with the people? Power can’t be such a bad thing.

Anubhuti: I agree. I was thinking about this too and I pored through the constitutional debates to find out why our Constitution took this form. I read the speech Dr. Ambedkar gave when he presented the first draft of the Constitution in the parliament. He had very clear answers to why we chose this form of government and this form of constitution.

Before we delve into that, I want to share my thoughts on the question of power. Why is it so critical and why should we worry about it? The answer to your question is in the question itself. It is true that democracy primarily means individual citizens have the power to control the State. This means equal power to all citizens irrespective of their identities and ideological inclinations. But, in a country as diverse as ours, we need a multi-party democracy; and it is inevitable that parties will represent different groups of peoples with particular inclinations, agendas, and even ideologies. Parties do claim to represent particular sections of the polity – either regions, or caste, or religions, and therefore particular ideologies.

If we want all of them to share equal power, there is a risk that if the population of some groups is more than that of others, through elections, more representatives of those groups will form governments. They will end up having a stronger say in policies and laws by the sheer power of numbers. Power will then mean that policies they want will be implemented, favouring their groups over others.

Ira: Hmmm. I think I get it. To ensure that one group does not end up controlling the State, we have multiple layers of representation like bicameralism at the central level, and also individual states have the option to choose bicameral legislatures. That way, by design, power ends up being distributed at multiple levels. Your explanation of the Rajya Sabha clarified this point.

Horizontally also, power is distributed by separating the legislature from the executive and judiciary. It would help to see the governance structures like a matrix, with the distribution of responsibilities and power, both horizontally and vertically.

In principle, we citizens have power over other groups, which we exercise through our votes, and through civil society. That implies vertical power which becomes dangerous – majority votes for a particular party, therefore exercising their power over minorities.

Anubhuti: Right. You see why the governance structure is so complex? It is by design. Ambedkar very eloquently and powerfully explained this by comparing the parliamentary system with the presidential system, which the USA has chosen. In his speech, Ambedkar raised the basic question – What is the form of government that is envisaged in the Constitution? His answer was that we chose a parliamentary system and not a presidential one.

In our system, the president is the head of the State but not the executive. The president represents the nation. In the US, the president rules the executive. They have secretaries in charge of various departments, but the president is not bound by their advice and can even dismiss them. In India, the president is generally bound by the advice of the ministers, who represent the people.

Ira: Yes, you had shared this before when we spoke about federalism. I still don’t understand the logic behind this way of organizing the State. What is the purpose of having the same position – that of the president – but with a different kind of role?

Anubhuti: Dr. Ambedkar explained this in the following manner. He said a democratic executive (elected government) must satisfy two conditions – stability and responsibility. There should be a good balance of both conditions. The American system seems to value stability over responsibility, which means the senate is a non-parliamentary executive, they are not dependent on a majority in the parliament (or congress, as it is called in the US). Since they are voted differently, the US parliament cannot dismiss the senate. They are a stable government.

On the other hand, in India, we have a parliamentary government – which means, the ministers are all part of the party that enjoys a majority. But, they can still be dismissed by the legislature through a no-confidence motion, so they have to be more responsible; but the government could be called less stable since they are questioned regularly in the parliament.

For India, Ambedkar said, a daily or more frequent assessment of the executive – the ruling government – was necessary. He said, “The Draft Constitution in recommending the Parliamentary system of Executive has preferred more responsibility to more stability.”[i]

Ira: Hmm. I am not sure I understand the American system and its logic, but I get what you are saying about India. You did say earlier that the executive is controlled within the legislature by the opposition, the judiciary, and the Rajya Sabha, and outside by the civil society, media, and the general population.

Anubhuti: I found his answer to the second question – what is the form of constitution – equally, or rather more interesting.

Ira: What did he have to say about the form of the constitution? I am not sure I understand what that question means.

Anubhuti: By form of the constitution, he meant whether it is a unitary or a federal constitution, which, he says, are the two principal forms of constitutions known to history.

Ira: But we have spoken at length about federalism. Is there more to this than you have already said?

Anubhuti: You are right. I did describe federalism earlier, but what I found interesting in Dr. Ambedkar’s speech is the fundamental principle behind why we designed federalism in this manner, and how it is different from most other forms of federalism, especially the one in the USA.

Notably, unlike the USA, which has dual citizenship, in India we have a singular citizenship. In the US, each person is the citizen of a particular state and a citizen of the nation.

States can discriminate in favour of their own citizens in matters of right to vote, right to hold public office, and even have access to public offices or employment or practising law in some cases. A state has the right to offer jobs or access to public schools only to local citizens, facilities that determine whether they are citizens of that state. Obviously, this is not the case in India. We are only citizens of India, so we have the same rights anywhere in the country. Article 19(e) of the Constitution gives us the Right to movement – live anywhere in the country, so we should have the same rights anywhere in India. In the US, states can even grant certain rights to their own citizens, but not to citizens of other states. They have different laws in different states. For instance, gambling is legal in some states in the US, but illegal in others.

Ira: Wow! That is rather extreme, isn’t it? Imagine having different laws in different states. We will have to keep track of where we are before we decide what law to follow and what its implications are. I watched this movie in which two boys from New York are charged with murder in a different state, where murder is punishable by death.

Anubhuti: In India, we have dual polity but single citizenship. Dual polity means we have to vote for the state assembly elections as citizens of a particular state, and vote for Lok Sabha elections as citizens of India. But we have only one passport which identifies us as citizens of India.

Anyway, enough of the USA. What I found interesting was Ambedkar’s analysis of federalism. He said it is usually accused of being too weak or effete as it suffers from two issues – rigidity and legalism.

By rigidity he meant that federalism is complex and so demands a written constitution. And once it is written, it becomes less flexible. Any change or interpretation requires significant effort and also judicial intervention. We discussed flexibility last time when we were talking about amendments to the Constitution.

Legalism simply means that once written, if there is any interference by the centre in issues that are the responsibilities of the states then that would be a breach of the Constitution and is justiciable. For instance, if the central government makes any law or policy on issues in the concurrent list or state list, a state can take them to the supreme court.

Ira: Now that is mind boggling. Democracy is indeed too much about power.

Anubhuti: In order to overcome this rigidity, the authority of the Indian parliament is extended to many matters which are in the concurrent list. More importantly, amendments to some parts of the Constitution are dependent on whether states are in agreement. The most important example of this is the distribution of the legislative powers between the centre and the states (items in the concurrent list).

Of course, the most important feature of Indian federalism is that we have uniform laws across the nation – all three kinds, criminal, civil, and family laws.

Ira: Ok, ok. This is becoming an overload of information now. I know more about federalism and its complexities than I cared to know.

But jokes aside, I do understand the connection between fear of power and how we should worry more about that than the complexities of governance structures.

I have also been wondering how much, if at all, the Constitution was influenced by the social and political realities of the time. The partition of India and the exit of the British who left behind remnants of the Government of India Act, 1935. People usually say the Constitution was influenced by post-colonialism. What do you think of that?

Anubhuti: I don’t really know. I have mixed thoughts about it. The social realities were dealt with through rigorous examination of principles like Ambedkar explained in this speech. But still we do have different family laws based on religion, special rights to minorities, and some others which seem like exceptions to the rule.

Maybe we should talk about that another time. It requires some more thought…and I suspect, some more reading.

[i]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/04-nov-1948/

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum Theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
How does Indian federalism differ from that of the USA? Prakash Iyer writes about the history and principles behind India’s governance structure.
